text: ALFAIATES, a fortified v. of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 25 m. SSE of Pinhel.

ALFAQUES, or San Carlos, a good natural harbour of Spain, in the prov. of Tarragona, on an island at the mouth of the southernmost branch of the Ebro, 16 m. S of Tortosa. It has an extent of 6 m. E and W; with a depth of from 3 to 4 fathoms. A canal was begun from Amposta on the r. bank of the Ebro to this place, with the view of evading the shoals and rapids of the Ebro, but the work was never finished, or was so imperfectly executed as to prove of no service to navigation.
ALFARO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Soria, at the confluence of the Alhama and Ebro, 11 m. E by S of Calahorra. Pop. 4,800.
ALFDORF, a v. of Wurtemburg, in the circle of Jaxt, 9 m. NW of Gemünd. Pop. 1,230.
ALFEDENA, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra Prima, on the Sangro, 20 m. SE of Sulmona. Pop. 1,645.
ALFELD. See AHLFELD.
ALFEN. See ALPHEN.
ALFENS, a small river of Tyrol, flowing into the Ill on the r. bank.
ALFENDEGA-DA-FE, a v. of Portugal, in the prov. of Traz-os-Montes, on the Zacharras, 14 m. N of Moncorvo. Pop. 1,000.

ALFEO, or Anapus, a river of Sicily, rising in Mount Laura, and flowing into the bay of Syracuse, near the ruins of the ancient city of that name. It receives a branch—"a clear and rapid stream" [Hoare] —from the fountain of Cyane.
ALFKARLEBY, a v. of Sweden, at the mouth of the Dal-elf, in the gov. of Upsala.
ALFODEN, a town of Norway, in the prov. of North Bergen, 98 m. NNE of Bergen.
ALFOLD, a p. of Surrey, 82 m. E of Godalming. Area 2,610 acres. Pop. 519.
ALFONSAO, a small island to the NE of Madagascar, in S lat. 7° 3½', E long. 52° 20½'. It is about 3 m. in length from N to S.

ALFONTES, a town of Portugal, in Algarve, 18 m. ESE of Silvas. ALFORAS, ALFU'RAS, or HARAFO'RAS, a savage race of people —the Alfoers and Alforese of the early navigators—inhabiting the interior of New Guinea, New Britain, and New Ireland. They seem to resemble the native Australians in their physical character and appearance; and are with them collectively termed Alforas by Pritchard. Lesson—who calls them Endamênes—says that those he saw in New Guinea had a repulsive physiognomy, with flat noses, projecting cheek-bones, large eyes, prominent teeth, long and slender legs, black thick hair nowise woolly, and coarse thick beards. Their colour was a deep dirty brown or black. Leyden [Asiatic Researches, x. 217] says the Haraforas are "indigenous in almost all the Eastern isles, and are sometimes found on the same island with the Papuas or Oriental Negroes. They are often," he adds, "lighter in colour than the Mahommedan races, and generally excel them in strength and activity." Stavorinus, too, says the few which he saw of this nation appeared to him not so dark in colour, and both handsomer and more sinewy than the Amboynese. He gives an account of them from a manuscript by Rumphius preserved in the archives strong, and savage people, in general taller than the inhabitants of the sea-shores," and inhabiting the wild mountains and interior parts of Ceram. "They go mostly naked," both men and women, the manuscript continues, " and only wear a thick bandage round their waist which is called chiaaca, and is made of the milky bark of a tree called by them sacka. They tie their hair upon the head over a cocoa-nut shell, and stick a comb in it. Round the neck they wear a string of beads. Their arms are a sword made of bamboo, together with a bow and arrows. They are sharp-sighted, and so nimble in running that they can run down and kill a wild hog at its utmost speed. No one is allowed amongst them to take a wife before he can show a head of an enemy which he has cut off. . . . Their women are of a tolerably fine complexion, wellproportioned, and altogether by no means disagreeable." [Voyage to the East Indies.]

ALFORD, a p. and t. in Lincolnshire. Area of p. 1,410 acres. Pop. 1,945.—The town is on a small stream which flows into the German ocean, 5 m. from its mouth, and 7 m. NE of Spilsby. A canal from this place to Anderby has been projected. It gives the title of viscount to the family of Brownlow .- Also a small p. in Somersetshire on the Brue rivulet, <math>1\frac{3}{4}</math> m. SW of Castlecary. Area 710 acres. Pop. 90.- Also a p. and t. in Aberdeenshire, 15 m. W of Inverury, on the banks of the Don, over which there is here a bridge of three arches, with a water-way of 128 ft. It is between 7 and 8 m. in length from SW to NE, and varies from 3 to 5 in breadth; containing nearly 8,000 Scotch acres. Pop. in 1801, 644; in 1841, 1,037.—Also a division of Aberdeenshire comprising 7 parishes, with a pop. in 1841 of 12,091. The rocks of this district are chiefly granite, and rise, in Caillievar, to the height of 1,480 ft.—Also a township in Berkshire, co. Massachusetts, U. S., 14 m. S by W of Lenox. Pop. 481.

ALFORT, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Seine, at the confluence of the Seine and Marne, on the r. bank of the latter stream, 5 m. SE of Paris, celebrated for its veterinary school which was founded here in 1766 by Bertin, minister-of-finance under Louis XV. Pop. 700.
ALFRED, a township in York, co. Maine, U. S., 24 m. N by W of York. Pop. 1,408.—Also a township in Alleghany co., New York, 12 m. SE of Angelica. Pop. 1,630.

ALFRETON, a p. and market-town of Derbyshire. Area of the p. 4,550 acres. Pop. 7,577.—The t. is 16 m. NNE of Derby. The manufacture of stockings and coarse pottery, and several extensive collieries and iron-works, afford employment to the inhabitants. The North Midland railway passes A. within 1 m.
ALFRISTON, a p. in Sussex, 2 m. N of Seaford. Area 2,120 acres. Pop. 668.
ALFTAVATA, a sheet of water in Iceland which connects the Thingvalla lake with the river Sog.
ALGAIDA, a barren district of Spain, in Andalusia, between Bonanza and San Lucar,—a mere expanse of swamps and sandy tracts clothed with a scanty vegetation.
ALGAJOLA, a town of Corsica, at the mouth of the Aregno, on the NW coast, 6 m. NE of Calvi. Pop. 259.
ALGAMESI, or ALGEMISIA, a town of Spain, in Valentia, on the road from Alcira to Valentia, 18 m. S of the latter place.
ALGAR (CAPE), a headland on the NW coast of Majorca, 5 leagues NW of Palma.—Also a v. of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Cadiz, 24 m. ENE from Xeres de la Frontera.
ALGARINEJO, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Granada, between Loja and Montefrio. It is seated in a fertile district watered by tributaries of the Jenil or Genil.
ALGARKIRK, a p. in Lincolnshire, 8 m. NNE of Spalding. Area 6,050 acres. Pop. 754.

ALGARROVA (PUNTA DE), the N point of the bay of Mayaguez in the island of Porto Rico, in N lat. 18° 14', W long. 67° 7½'.-Also a headland on the ALG NE coast of Hayti, 13 m. ESE ½ E from Cape Isabella, the extreme N point of the island. It is sometimes called Casrouge Point.
ALGARROVO, a river of New Granada, flowing NNW to the Cauca, in the district of Santa Martha. —Also a town of Spain, in the prov. of Granada, 161 m. E of Malaya, and within 2 m. of the coast.

ALGARVE, the most southernly and the smallest of the six prov. of Portugal. It is bounded on the N by Alemtejo; on the E by the Guadiana, which separates it from the Spanish prov. of Andalusia; and on the S and SW by the Atlantic ocean. It lies between 36° 56' and 37° 56' N lat., taking the extreme S point at Cape Santa Maria, the southern extremity of three low islands off the coast; and between 9° and 8° 46' W long. Its extent along the S coast, from the mouth of the Guadiana to Cape St. Vincent in a direct line is 100 m; and its breadth varies from 16 to 35 m. Its total superficies is estimated at 2,099 sq. m. It is traversed in its northern districts by the Serra de Monchique, running from W to E, of which the highest summit—73 leagues to the NE of Cape St. Vincent—attains an alt. of 3,830 ft., and which to a great extent separates it from the adjacent prov. of Alemtejo; and by the Serra de Caldeirao, which inclines towards the SE. Ramifications of the Guadiana-Sagres, and Guadiana-San-Vincente, run towards the SW, and terminate in the lofty promontories from which these mountains derive their names. These wild and barren ranges are separated by narrow valleys from a chain of low but steep limestone ridges, uncultivated and covered with loose stones, which extend along the S coast from Cape St. Vincent to Tavira, leaving only a narrow strip of cultivated land between them and the sea, on which are built most of the towns and villages of A. The prov. is watered by numerous mountain-streams, but with the exception of the Guadiana, it possesses no river of importance. Some mineral springs occur. The coastwhich forms several bays, affording excellent harbourage-is from its SW extremity to the point of Pudade, in W long. 8° 37', bold and rocky, rising in Cape St. Vincent, which forms the SW extremity of Europe, and in Point Sagres, a league to the SE of it, to the height of 80 ft.; thence to the mouth of the Guadiana it is flat and sandy. Towards the interior, the soil is stony and in many places very scanty; but it is generally well-cultivated, and produces figs, almonds, oranges, lemons, citron, dates, pomegranates, grapes and algarrobas (Ceratonia edulis) in the highest perfection. Olives are likewise abundant but of inferior quality. The best wines-all of which are white—are those of Lagos, Villa-nova de Portimao, and Alvor. The principal supply of corn is derived from Spain; the bread of this prov., however, is considered the best in Portugal. Herbage is scanty: the principal sustenance of the numerous goats which are reared in A. consists of marine plants. Evergreen oaks, cork-trees, chestnuts, and kermes, clothe the mountains; and mines of silver and copper occur in its SW district. The commerce consists in dried fruits, kermes, and salt, derived from the sea-marshes in the neighbourhood of Castro Marim. With the exception of Greece, Algarve is the only country in which caprification, or the art of ripening the domestic fig by puncturing, is practised. The industrial occupations of the inhabitants consist almost exclusively in fishing and agriculture. As mariners they are unrivalled by any of their countrymen. The only manufacture which the prov. possesses is that of linen. The climate of A. is mild in winter, and in summer it is tempered by the sea-breeze; and, except in the marshy districts on the coast, it is salubrious. The periodical phenomenon of the concurrence of the direction of the wind with the course of the suncalled by the Algarvines Vento Rodeiro-is peculiar to this locality.—The prov. is divided into 3 comarcas or districts, Lagos, Faro, and Tavira, which derive their names from their chief towns. Lagos, situated on the bay of the same name, is properly the cap. of Algarve, though the residence of the governor is at Tavira. The coast is studded with fortresses, the principal of which are Sagres, Lagos, and Castro Marin. The number of inhabitants was estimated in 1802 at 127,615; in 1835 at 135,260. These numbers, however, are mere approximations founded on the number of hearths or fire-places. Algarve, which derives its name from an Arabic word signifying 'the West,' still retains the title of kingdom. It formerly comprised a great extent of territory, extending into Spain as far as Almeira, and along the northern coast of Africa.

ALGAS, or ALGUAS, a river of Spain, rising 30 m. SSE of Alcaniz, and flowing by a NW and then NNE course to the Ebro. It is joined by the Nonaspe, on the l. bank, at the town of that name; and throughout its entire course of about 50 m., separates Aragon on the W, from Catalonia on the E.
ALGAU, a name sometimes given to that portion of Suabia which lies on the E side of the Bodensee.

ALGAUCIN, a town of Spain, in the Sierra de Ronda of Granada, on the ridge of a hill overlooking the river Guadiro, whence a fine glacis, covered with vineyards and corn-fields, extends to the valleys on either side. Jacob estimates the pop. at 10,000.

ALGEBERANA HEAD, a rugged promontory on the N coast of Brazil, known also as the RETEIRO Grande, in S lat. 4° 36', W long. 37° 30\frac{1}{2}'. The land to the SE of this point, for a distance of 3 or 4 m., forms two distinct terraces, the lower of which has a red colour, while the upper is grey.

ALGECIRAS, or ALGEZIRAS, a maritime city of Spain, in Andalusia, on the W side of the bay of Gibraltar, 10 m. NE of Tarifa, and 6 m. sailing distance W of Gibraltar, or 10 m. by land around the head of the bay; in N lat. 36° 8', W long. 5° 26'. [Conn. des Temps, 1848.] It is built partly on a flat along the beach, and partly on the slope of small heights which form the front elevation towards a ridge of mountains extending in a SW direction from the mountains of Granada to the straits near Tarifa, and having a general elevation of 2,000 ft. It is comparatively a modern town, having been chiefly built in 1760 by Charles III., on the capture and continued possession of Gibraltar by the English. The streets are "inconveniently narrow, but the houses, being generally built of stone, have a better look than the mud-walled and straw-thatched houses so common in the western provinces of Spain." [Semple.] It is described by Ford as a "rectangular commonplace" town with a pop. of about 16,000; but this estimate of the pop. is certainly too high. Möller, in 1840, assigns to it only 4,000; the Penny Cyclopedia, in 1833, gives it 9,900. The inhabitants, in their manners, are said to retain strong traces of the Moors; but the principal part of the pop. of A. is drawn from Andalusia. It has a harbour sheltered by a mole, which admits coasting-vessels at high water, and a dockyard. Its principal legitimate trade is the export of coal, which is wrought in the neighbouring mountains; some charcoal is also exported; but its ence, near the l. bank of the Rhine, 6 m. W of Maymost lucrative trade is a contraband one with its English neighbours on the other side of the bay. A packet-boat used to sail twice a-week from A. for the port of Ceuta, belonging to Spain, on the opposite coast of Africa: the voyage was usually performed in three or four hours. About half-a-mile to the SE of the mole lies the Isla Verde or Green isle; and 23 m. to the N of this isle, near the head of the bay, is the mouth of the Palmones, which is resorted to in win- ALG ter by the trading vessels of A. A small stream called El Miel, rises in the Trocha or mountains behind A., in two streams, and passing the town on the W and S side, flows into the bay between the town and a few houses, the remains of the original town founded by the Moors, and known as the Villa Veija or 'Old town.'-A. was built by the Moors, who held it, as their key to Spain, for upwards of six centuries. After a notable siege of twenty months, it was taken by Alonso XI., aided by the chivalry of Europe, in 1344. The governorship of A. is one of the best appointments under the crown of Spain, being valued at £5,000 a-year. His principal duty is to keep a watchful eye on the formidable British stronghold on the opposite side of the bay, and on the narrow entrance of the Mediterranean. In the subjoined sketch of the bay of Algeçiras which is formed to the eastward by the promontory of Gibraltar, with its sandy isthmus, and to the westward by the bases of the mountains behind A., Europa point is at A; the town of Gibraltar at B; the Spanish lines across the peninsula at ccc. A flat coast, backed by several small heights, forms the head of the bay. On one of these heights is placed the small town of San Roque; on another, El Rocadillo, r, are still faintly discernible, amidst corn-fields, a few relics of the ancient city of Carteia, the Melcarth of the Phænicians. A few miles to the W of San Roque, we cross the Guadaranca, d d, in a ferry-boat moved by a rope. A flat of about 2 m., intersected by ditches and causeways, brings us to the mouth of the Palmones, e e e, which flows past the town of Los Barrios, and is also crossed by a ferry. If the tide be low, we may continue our route along the sands, till we climb a low ridge along which the town of Algerias, D, is partly built. Descending from this ridge, we pass along the head of a small bay, and then, climbing the hills, fall into the wild, rocky, romantic path which leads to Tarifa, the most southern point of Europe. The Isla Verde is marked f in our sketch; the Spanish quarantine house, g; Cabrita point, <math>h</math>; and Punta Frayle, <math>m</math>. On the western side of the bay, a vessel may anchor off Cabrita point in from 18 to 27 fath. But the most secure anchorage lies from off the centre of the town of A. northwards towards the mouth of the Palmones.—Bourgoing.— Semple.—Ford.
ALGERI. See ALGHERO.
ALGESHEIM, or GANALGESHEIM, OF INGELHEIM, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the circle of Mayence. Pop. 2,000.
ALGESUR, or ALJESUR, a small town of Portugal, on the W coast of Algarve, at the mouth of a small stream which flows into the sea between the bay of Arrifana on the S, and Cape Sardao on the N.
ALGEZIRA. See DIYARBEKIR.
ALGHA, one of the Andreanof group, in the Aleutian chain of islands.

ALGHERO, a city on the W coast of Sardinia, in the prov. and 15 m. S by W of Sassari; in N lat. 40° 26', E long. 8° 161'. Pop. 6,700. It is built on a low rocky point, jutting out from a sandy beach, in the form of a parallelogram, with stout walls flanked by bastions and towers.

ALGIERS. Or Algeria, as it is commonly called by its new rulers the French-a country of Northern Africa, one of the Barbary states, and for upwards of three centuries a subordinate part of the Turkish empire: bounded on the N by the Mediterranean; on the E by the regency of Tunis; on the S, for a distance of nearly 500 m., by the Sahara, or Great desert; and on the W by the empire of Marocco. The exact boundaries of this territory are still as undefined as they were during the period of Turkish domination. Many of the tribes nominally included within the sway of its rulers have never conceded their independence; the southern districts are almost unknown to geographers, and the dey's authority was little felt in them. Towards Marocco, the mountains of Trara, and the river Mulyva, or Mulluiah, and further to the S the desert of Angad, form something of a natural although not very precise line of demarcation; and, notwithstanding the long existence of European establishments in Tunis, the frontiers between that state and Algeria have not been determined with any greater certainty. Capt. Berard makes the boundary line on the side of Tunis strike the coast about 4 m. to the E of La Calle. The French military charts have successively advanced the limits of Algiers in this quarter from the brook of Saint-Martin, near Calle, to the Wad-el-Zayne, 12 leagues further to the E, and latterly to a line drawn along the courses of the Kef, the Tibessah, and the Tuggurt; while M. Baude, in his work on Algeria, contends that the eastern limits of A. ought to be defined by a line drawn from Cape Roux along the mountains of Kumir, whose impassable acclivities form a natural boundary between the two states. Still greater difficulties exist in determining the breadth of Algeria from N to S, although the French troops have penetrated at different points to the northern frontier of the Sahara. The greatest breadth of the territory appears to be on the meridian of Constantine. Perhaps we shall not greatly err in taking the extent of this country from E to W, or from the Zayne on the side of Tunis to Twunt on the western frontier, at the foot of the mountains of Trara, at 500 m., and its average breadth at 100 m. Shaler, however, estimates the superficial area at only 30,000 sq. m. Coast.] The extent of the bay of Algiers from E to W is 12 m.; and the depth of its embrasure about 6 m. Its eastern side is formed by Cape Matifu, a low promontory about 2 m. in length; and the western by Cape Caxine, of which the highest point is Abu Zaryah, and Point Pescade the most northern projection. It has 40 fathoms water within the line of the two capes; and shoals to 15 and 10 near the head. As a harbour it affords but little shelter in winter. On a commanding position, at the distance of about a mile from this port, is the fort of Bab- Azum. Thence to Cape Bingut, a distance of 36 m. ESE # E, the coast affords neither shelter nor anchorage. About 16 m. ESE 1/2 E of Cape Bingut is the port of Dellyz, defended by the long narrow promontory of the same name. Further to the E is the lofty Cape Corbelin, remarkable for its reddish colour and the striature of its rocky structure. To the S of this cape is the mountain of Azafun, 4,460 ft. in height. From Cape Sigli, named by Shaw Ash-oune-mou-kar, the Vabar of Ptolemy, 33 m. ESE of Cape Dellyz, a lofty range of perpendicular rocks runs nearly ESE to Cape Carbon, which forms the NE extremity of a barren rocky mass, the summit of which, named Goureya, rises 2,200 ft. above the level of the sea. After describing various sinuosities, the line of coast forms the bay which contains the town of Bugia, and affords excellent harbourage at all periods of the year. From Cape Cavallo, and the small islands of the same name, a series of low level rocks runs to the port of Jijeli or Zeyeli, 30 m. E by S of Cape Carbon, which is similar to but smaller and less secure than that of Tripoli. Sheltered on the S and E by the land, and to a considerable extent on the N by a line of rocks, it is capable of being rendered an eligible winter-harbour. Cape Bugaroni, so named by the Genoese coral-fishers, or Sebba Rous, [i.e. 'the Seven Capes,'] sometimes also called Ras-al-Friganah, extends a distance of nearly 16 m. from E to W, and rises to a height of 3,576 ft. Its most northern point, and the most northerly point of Algeria, is Ras-al-Zhura, 32 m. E & N from Point Jijeli, in N lat. 37° 6', E long. 6° 26', thence to Ras-al-Friganah, a distance of 6 m., the coast trends E by S. The bay of Kolah or Collo, with the rocky peninsula of Aldjerda, is extremely varied and picturesque. Beyond it is the narrow point of Ras Bibi, and a rocky coast terminating in a bay opening to the deep and woody valley of Akmes. Further to the SE is a large cape without a name, extending in the direction of the island of Irigina, and the little bay of Stora, considered by the Moors the safest port in the regency. The low sandy cape of Skikida or Rusicada, a projection about 6 m. in length, is succeeded by the rocky crested Cape Tilfila; and nearly on a parallel with and at the distance of 36 m. from Cape Bugaroni, is Ras Hadid or Cape Ferro, in N lat. 37° 5', E long. 7° 10', composed of bare masses of grit rising to the height of 1,574 ft. To the S of Cape Ferro is Ras Arxin; and about 3 m. further on is a little creek, the extremity of a deep ravine, supposed to be the Sullucu of the Peutingerian table, or the Collops magnus of Ptolemy. A wall of rocks running NE terminates in the conical-shaped Voile noire; beyond which is Cape Garde or Ras-el-Hamrah, a prolongation of a branch of Mount Edough. After presenting various sinuosities on a line running generally ESE or SE, the coast turns NE to Cape Rosa, which, at the distance of 51 m. from Ras Hadid, rises to the height of 300 ft. close on the coast, but has an elevation of 1,080 ft. in the interior. The coral banks of Algiers exist at this point. Between Capes Garde and Rosa is the gulf of Bona, in N lat. 36° 54', E long. 7° 46'. From Cape Rosa to Cape Roux the coast-district is slightly elevated, and generally covered with thick brushwood; beyond, it becomes very steep, and terminates in Cape Tabarca, forming a large shallow bay, at the opening of which is the island of the same name, in N lat. 36° 58', E long. 8° 46', a sterile rock surmounted with fortifications. Proceeding westwards from the bay of Algiers beyond Ras Akkonadda, is a large bay, terminated by the peninsula of Sidi-el-Ferruch, in N. lat. 36° 46', W long. 2° 51'. At Ras-el- Amusch, 35 m. from Ras Akkonadda, the coast becomes elevated, rising in its principal summit to a height of 2,090 ft. The port of Sharshel or Zarzel, situated in a small circular bay opening to the NE, is now available only for small boats. Ras Tenez or Cape Nakkus, in N lat. 36° 33', E long. 1° 23', 48 m. from Ras-el- Amusch, consists of huge perpendicular masses of rock, extending from E to W over a surface of 3 m. At the distance of about half-a-mile from this cape is the island of Colombi or Palomas, a small rock 85 ft. high. Beyond this the coast forms a long shallow curve, designated by Shaw the bay of Magrowa. Cape Ivi is a low point of land; but behind it, at a little distance, the mountains of the Shelif rise to the height of 740 ft. From the rocky point of Shelif to the bay of Arzew, 57 leagues from Algiers, the outline of the coast presents little variety; this bay affords excellent anchorage at all seasons of the year. The group of mountains between Arzew and the bay of Oran, rising to a height of 2,050 ft., terminates in Cape Ferrat and Cape Abuja, which are 8 m. distant from each other. Point Canastel, 7 m. SW 1/4 W from Cape Abuja, forms the NE point of the bay of Oran; and at the distance of 5 m. SE 1 E is Cape Falcon, the other extremity of the bay, in N lat. 35° 46', W long. 0° 47'. Hence westwards to the river Mulyva or Mulluiah, forming the boundary with Marocco, the coast is desert and featureless, and the different points of land succeed each other as follow: At 16 m. W by S from Cape Falcon is Cape Sigale; 10 m. SW 3 S from Cape Sigale is Cape Figali; from the latter cape to Cape Guardia is a distance of 29 m. W 3 S; from Cape Guardia to Cape Hunyham is 18 m.; and thence to the mouth of the Mulyva, in N lat. 35° 5′, W long. 2° 14′, is 12 m. Mountain - system.] Algeria is intersected in its entire length by different parallel chains of the Atlas, forming ramifications of the principal ridges, ridges, and, in some instances, isolated groups. These mountain-masses-which extend over nearly the entire surface of the country, and constitute its prominent feature - are commonly divided, although with little definiteness or appropriateness of distinction, into two leading chains, which are distinguished by the names of the Great and the Little Atlas. The former, which is the most southerly, bordering on the Sahara, extends from Cape Ghir on the E, to the gulf of Khabes on the W.; terminating in the lower ranges which encircle the Great Syrtis. Its greatest heights-which are called the High Atlas-lie between Marocco and Fez, beginning about 30 m. to the S of Marocco, and extending to Djebel d' Zatoute, the ancient Abila, near Choukta. The lofty rugged Wannashrees belongs to this range, and its snowcapped summits form an important land-mark in the SW of Algeria. - The Little Atlas, or Maritime Atlas, as it is sometimes called, rising near Tangier, stretches along the coast of Barbary, across Marocco, to the prov. of Titteri in the SE of Algeria; and according to M. Fournel, terminates in the Auress, the loftiest summit of the Atlas mountains. This chain is composed of numerous and diversified ranges, generally not very elevated, and, except on the steep acclivities and loftier peaks, is covered with fruit or forest trees. —These two principal ranges are connected by many transverse ridges, generally of inferior elevation. Towards the eastern extremity of the mountainous plateau called by Ritter the Central Atlas, the massive Jurjura rises to a great height. Geology.] The Atlas mountains are characterized by precipitous rocky ridges, and the immense fissures into which their structures are rent, from their bases upwards to their peaks. They consist of enormous accumulations of the transition, secondary, and tertiary series, volcanic formations, diluvium, and the various formations of the later geological epoch. Of primary rocks the northern parts of Africa seem in a great measure destitute. A schistose talc, in horizontal beds running to the S, very brilliant, and frequently passing into well-characterized mica schist, composes the principal mass of the transition formation. At one point near the coast, the schist is gradually combined with felspar, while the talc passes into mica, and the mass becomes welldefined gneiss. The region of the schistose group extends over a distance of 400 m. Its stratification is very irregular. The mountain-ridges have rounded summits; their elevations are abrupt; and they are separated by deep valleys forming the channels of numerous small streams which become dry in summer.—The stratification of the gneiss is equally irregular; and it contains no organic remains. The mountains of this formation are inferior in elevation to the schistose ranges; they have few springs, and little vegetation. The mountain-masses of the little Atlas, especially to the S of the great plain of the Metidja, are principally composed of a schistose marl resembling the European lias, alternating with strata of calcareous marl. On the S declivity of Beni-Sala it is reported to pass into real slate. Organic remains are very rare in these formations, and consist chiefly of oyster and scallop shells: the lamellations do not even present any vegetable impressions. Mineral productions are equally scarce in the calcareous clay formations of the Atlas; but to the S of Mount Teniah copper is abundant. The mountains of this formation are lofty but not very steep. The two main ridges of the chain enclose deep and narrow valleys; and innumerable fissures are worn in the clay by the mountain-torrents.-Towards the N, the tertiary subatlantic range occurs only in fragments; but the entire mass of the Central Atlas consists of this formation, which is identical with that which occurs on both sides of the Apennines. It is of two epochs: the oldest being a blue clay covered with strata of calcareous freestone, alternating with yellow or red grit. The second series contains only a few inconsiderable veins of iron, and some traces of galena or lead-ore. This formation is best developed on the coast, towards Oran, where it forms the basis of the great plain which extends to the E of that city; it occurs also in the mountains of Ramra, at an elevation of 1,540 ft. above sea level; and it constitutes the chain skirting the road to Tlemson. The second series differs in some respects from the Algerine. It consists of alternate layers of clay and limestone to the thickness of from 100 to 140 ft. Whitish cretaceous limestone occupies the lower position; then come beds of a coarse tertiary limestone, alternating with a yellowish and sometimes with a reddish sand, with intermediate strata of large oyster and cockle shells. The upper portion consists of a calcareous breccia. This range, on the Algerine side, exhibits little vegetation; while towards Oran the soil of many of the plains is extremely fertile. But it is remarked, that the tertiary range of mountains which border on the sea are much more favourable to vegetation than those nearer the interior. Few volcanic rocks have been discovered in Algeria. In the environs of Calle the Abbe Poiret detected evident traces of ancient volcanoes. Between Oran and the fort of Mers-el-Kebir, schistose rocks of a bluish grit occur, which appear to have been thrust by volcanic action into the position which they now occupy. At Cape Matifou, near the coast, trachytic grit, a species of porphyry, proceeds from the tertiary series; and diluvium deposit composes the soil of the plain of Metidja, in a series of horizontal beds of argillaceous gritty marl and round gravel. The character of the marl is everywhere the same; but that of the gravel is often dissimilar. The alluvium of the Metidja contains no organic remains. The products of the most recent geological epoch are rarely developed on the coast of Barbary. Near the mouth of the Haratch, a few small downs appear.-Shaw, describing the environs of Tuggurt, mentions artesian wells which have been sunk by the inhabitants to the depth of from 100 to 200 fathoms, affording an abundant and constant supply of water; and both Arago and Fournel are of opinion, that a series of such wells might be carried across the desert from Biskra to Tuggurt. Fournel conceives, from the inflexions of the strata, that the space between Constantine and the mountains to the N of Merdjitel-Guzi on one side, and the plain of El-Kantara on the other, constitutes two artesian basins. The definitive inclination of the southern series, the density of the limestone which forms the higher strata, the porosity of the marl imbedded in the strata, afford, he says, strong reasons to believe that a series of borings would bring water to the surface here at any point; and there is good evidence that there exists under the Desert a sheet of water flowing from the Atlas, whose natural course would be from N to S. Hydrography. The hydrography also of Algeria is still very imperfectly known: only the lower parts of the rivers, which alone are navigable, have been delineated with accuracy. Numerous streams descend from the Little Atlas, and after flowing through a primordial valley of this chain, traverse the plain from N to S, then reaching the foot of the hills which rise between the Little Atlas and the coast, make a turn, sometimes very abrupt, and escape by openings to the sea. The principal rivers run E and W. The Mafragg—the Muthul of Sallust—has its mouth 5 leagues to the E of Bona, and intersects the plain in a direction nearly parallel to the Seibus or river of Bona, which river it equals in breadth and depth, and exceeds in navigable extent.—The upper part of the course of the Seibus—the Rubricatus of Ptolemy—is from NW to SE. Its left bank is skirted by the lower spurs of the mountains of Aouara and Talea; on the right lies the extensive plain of Ghelma. Emerging from the mountain-ranges which bound this plain on the S, it suddenly directs its course to Bona, near which it falls into the sea. The action of the east wind, obstructing the efflux of this river, has filled the space which formerly constituted one of the arms of the gulf of Bona with alluvium, and rendered the environs of the city itself extremely unhealthy.-The Bujimah, a small river whose current is very slow, loses part of its waters in the plain of Bona, and falls into the sea about half-a-mile from Bona.—The Wadel-Kebir or Rummel—the ancient Ampsaga or Flumius Cirtensis—after watering Constantine, is conducted by a deep valley to the sea near Cape Bugaroni. Some geographers identify this river with one named by Shaw Wad-Zun, whose mouth is situated further to the NE in the bay of Mers-el-Zeitun.—The Buberak, one of the largest rivers in Algeria, rises in the Zononah; under the name of the Nissah separates the provinces of Algeria and Constantine; and under that of the Wad-el-Kebir finds its way to the sea across a low sandy shore—through which it obtains unobstructed passage only during the rainy season—to the N of the mountains of Abdeluairet.—The Isser terminates its course through a low woody district on the opposite side of Mount Buberak.—The Hamise rises in the mountains of Beni Yaite, and falls into the bay of Algiers near Cape Matifu. This river is of inconsiderable extent: it is indeed never dry but is almost everywhere fordable. The district between the Hamise and Haratch is marshy and insalubrious. The latter river rises in the Little Atlas, intersects the plain of Metidja from N to S, and, after receiving the addition of several small streams, falls into the bay of Algiers. Except during the rainy season its estuary is generally obstructed by accumulations of sand.—The Maa-el-Zafran, one of the largest rivers of Algeria, is formed by the junction of the Shifaf and the Afrun or Jir, a little to the E of Koleah. The Shifaf is separated from the tributaries of the Afrun by a narrow ridge of land. Its banks are very elevated, especially to the E; those of the Jir are low and confined. These united rivers, formerly known by the name of Mazafran, pursue their course towards the E, in a channel of from 60 to 80 ft. in breadth, enclosed by high banks. After receiving the Buffarah, which issues from a gorge of the Atlas near Blidah, it falls precipitately into the sea.—The most considerable river in Algeria, for the length of its course and the volume of its waters, is the Shelif, whose sources, named Sebbein Ain-i. e. 'the Seventy fountains'—are at the foot of the Wannashrees. After running in an easterly direction for 40 m., it takes a northerly course for 50 m., and passing the city of Amoura runs for 120 m. in a direction nearly parallel to the coast, receiving, in this part of its course, the Har and several smaller streams, and flows without obstruction into the Mediterranean at a rocky point named the Point of Shelif.—The only other rivers of any importance are the Magta, called in the upper part of its course Habra; the Salato near Cape Fegalo, which is obstructed at the mouth by a bar of sand; and the Tafna, which falls into a little bay opposite the island of Areschgul or Harchgun.—The frontiers of the Desert are still too imperfectly known to afford any description of the rivers with which they are supplied from the reservoirs of the Great Atlas. Algeria possesses several lakes of considerable extent. To the S of the confines of the provinces of Algeria and Constantine is a sandy salt marsh called Al Shot. It is about 50 m. in length; and its medium breadth is estimated at 15 m. It covers an extensive plain, which is separated from the Sahara on the S by the mountains of Saada. The most important affluents of Lake Shot are the Bon-Saadah and the Ksab, which is formed by the union of the Benia and Sedjid.—Another salt lake called Melgig fills a basin of great magnitude in the district of Zaab on the E of Tuggurt, and receives the Wad Jidi or Adjidee .- Lake Titeri, situated between the provinces of Oran and Algeria, is crossed from N to S by the Shelif.— There are several lakes on the Mediterranean coast. The territory of Calle is girt with three lakes: two of which, the Tonegue or Tonga, and the Bastion, Salt Lake, or El Meleh, discharge themselves into the sea; the third, called El-Ubeirà—i. e. 'the Superior lake'—fills the greater part of the space between the two former. The last named lake is deep, and is connected with the sea by a channel of about 1,093 yards in length, which terminates in a creek capable of affording shelter to vessels of small dimensions. Its banks have been cleared of brush-wood, and present a gentle slope towards the water. The elevation of this lake is about 98 ft. above the Salt Lake or sealevel; and its distance from the former being only 2,200 yards, has suggested the idea of their junction by means of a canal.—About 13 m. SW of Bona, at the foot of Mount Edough is Lake Efzara, which covers an area of about 60 sq. m. This lake, which is very little above sea-level, connects itself during the rainy season with the sea by means of the valley of Kharezas which it inundates with its waters; and traces of a canal, designed by the Romans to prevent these inundations, still exist in the valley. About 2 m. to the S of the bay of Collo, is a lake running inland, but only separated from the sea by a bank of sand scarcely 300 ft. in height. This lake is supposed to have formerly been connected with the sea, and to have formed the beautiful and spacious harbour of Djebia.—In the environs of Oran are two lakes which become dry in summer. One of these, the Sebgha or Salt lake, situated in the plain near the city, extends several leagues from E to W; the other, El Melah, or the salines of Arzew, lies to the SE. Vegetation.] The mountain-ranges near the coast are covered with dense forests. Mount Boudjareah, an isolated mass extending over an area and presenting a superficial extent of 100,000 acres on the confines of the Metidja, affords beautiful specimens of the Aleppo pine. The oak and the cork-tree cover vast districts in the mountains of Blidah, Mascara, and Tlemsen. But it is in the neighbourhood of Calle that the forest-riches of Algeria are chiefly to be found. Here the alder, willow, elm, ash, maple, and Italian poplar, enrich the banks of the lakes of Tonga and Lake Superior; but the cork-tree generally predominates, its less combustible bark having preserved it from the ravages of Arab incendiaries. Another species of oak, called in Arabic zan or zanha, is produced in those districts of which the soil is dry. The vegetation of the Little Atlas closely resembles that of the S of Europe. The cactus and orange tree grow, on northern exposures, at an alt. of 1,800 ft.; but they do not sustain that elevation on a southern exposure. The fig is found at an elevation of 4,500 ft. The hills to the S of the Little Atlas, averaging about 3,200 ft. above sea-level, produce the olive-tree in scanty numbers, but neither the cactus nor orange. Towards the sea about two-thirds of the surface are covered with tall brushwood; marshy places, and the beds of streams are filled with the rose-laurel. The country to the S of the Little Atlas possesses little fertility, and in some parts presents complete sterility. The date-tree is here rare; but the dwarf-date is often found commingling with brushwood. The bitter orange and the citron grow wild in the vallies in the environs of Algiers. The pomegranate and arbutus are abundant in all parts of Barbary. The vine is indigenous, and is much cultivated in Algeria. Plantations of olive-trees are numerous and extensive, especially in the environs of Bugia, Tlemsen, and along the plain of the Metidja, at the foot of the Atlas. This vast plain, bounded by two mountain-ranges running nearly east and west, rises considerably towards the Little Atlas on the S, and presents extreme varieties of soil. A few scattered specimens of the famous mulberry plantations of the Spanish refugees still exist on the coast of Algiers. Wormwood grows here in great abundance. Umbelliferous plants attain considerable dimensions. The acanthus is constantly to be seen in the hedges. The cryptogamia are rare; there are few mosses or lichens; and the marine vegetation evinces compara tively little vigour. Zoology.] The lion still inhabits, as in ancient times, the recesses of the Numidian mountains and forests; and next to it the panther-for the tiger is not found in this country-holds sway in these savage districts. The leopard is also found in Algeria, and the tiger-cat, chati, serval, and hyæna rank among the beasts of prey; but the wolf is not thought to exist here, or indeed in any part of the African continent. Chacals are formidable from their numbers and their voracity. Wild boars inhabit the extensive oak-forests; and the brown bear has been seen in the higher regions of the Atlas. Monkeys and apes are numerous in the forests of the Lesser Atlas. The beautiful and graceful gazelle, and several other species of antelope, inhabit the chain of the Lesser Atlas from the frontiers of Tunis to the borders of Marocco. In the plain of the Metidja, great numbers of camels are reared; but the horse is the domestic animal in highest estimation amongst the natives; and the barbs, or Barbary horses, are of great beauty and excellence. The mules too are excellent.-Eagles, vultures, hawks, and owls of various species occur throughout Algeria. Snipes, curlews, plovers, storks, and herons are found in the marshy valleys; and on the sea-coast gulls, swifts, and various kinds of wading and swimming birds are met with. The ostrich is the well-known tenant of the desert.—Of reptiles the buskah and effah are serpents of a dangerous kind. Tortoises, turtles, and lizards abound. Fresh-water fishes are few in species; but the coasts of the Mediterranean abound in fish of all kinds.—Insects are numerous. Flies, fleas, bugs, and musquitoes fill the air; and locusts occasionally devastate the fields.—In zoophytes the coasts of Barbary are particularly rich, especially in corals. Sponges are also procured on the coast in the neighbourhood of the capital. Climate.] The cultivated parts of Algeria, especially the shores washed by the Mediterranean, and the northern declivities of the Little Atlas, enjoy an equable and delightful temperature. Numidia is famed in ancient story for its fine climate; and during the space of twelve years' residence in Algiers, Dr. Shaw knew the thermometer only twice at freezing point, nor did it ever rise to sultry heat unless when the wind blew direct from the Desert: the seasons passed insensibly into each other; and the whole variation of the barometer during these twelve years did not exceed 1.3 inches. There are generally three seasons: the temperate, which lasts from March to June; the hot, from July to November, when the ground is parched and the springs fail; and the rainy, which prevails from December to February. M. Rozet estimates the mean temp. in the city of A. at 62°; but during the months of July and August, a thermometer plunged in the sand has risen to 113° and even 122°. The temp. is highest during August. Dr. Wilde, who visited the city of A. in December 1837, considered the climate decidedly inferior to that of Madeira or of the Canaries. The daily temp. ranged during his stay from 57° at 9 A. M., to 63° at 3 P. M. The average mean temp. during the day was about 60°; but the nights were cold, falling as low as 54° at 9 P. M. The broken weather usually commences in November, and lasts at intervals till April. Mrs. Broughton records a heavy fall of snow and hail at A. on the 5th of March; but vegetation is generally in full activity in the middle of February. At high elevations, extreme cold and perilous snow-storms are frequently experienced. On the Algiers road to Aumale, between Tablat and Sakhamondi, an artillery convoy which had left Aumale on the 8th of January, 1848, was, on the third day, overtaken by a violent snow-storm on the heights in front of Sakhamondi. At the turnings of this perilous road, the burdened mules were precipitated into the deep ravines; and the cold became so intense, that in less than a quarter of an hour 14 men out of 44 fell beneath its severity. Those who were able to escape from this horrible situation took refuge in the dachera of Tifiras. At the same time that this convoy was suffering on the heights above mentioned, another from Algiers lost two men by the severity of the weather on the road to Ouled-el-Had-el-Tablat. Fogs are common in the plain, but not at Algiers. The prevalent winds are the N and NW. All the winds are violent at the equinoxes. [Smyth.] The question of climate is, however, still a doubtful one as regards the successful colonization of A. At Hamman, 2 leagues from Constantine, on the route to Philippeville, all the colonists who have attempted to locate themselves have died. Most of the hands employed in the government nursery and practical garden, 3 m. SE of Constantine, have also died or been disabled by a species of fever generated in the locality. Even the oldest French residents are periodically attacked by fever. [Correspondent of the Times.] Soil and Produce.] According to M. Desfontaines, the territory of A., with the exception of the parts bordering on the Desert, is less sandy and more fertile than that of Tunis. The prevailing soils of A. are light and ferruginous, as appears in their reddish tint. In some uncultivated districts, the detritus of the luxuriant natural vegetation has covered the surface with a rich humus or mould. In the plains, the soil is in some places black and loamy, in others it partakes of the prevailing light reddish-tinted character, but it is always highly productive to the N of Mount Atlas. The principal articles of cultivation are corn, barley, maize, millet, potatoes, melons, tobacco, madder, and henna. General Bugeaud says: - "In the short visits which I paid to Algeria in 1836 and 1837 I conceived but a poor idea of the fertility of the soil, so much vaunted by the ancients. Having passed over only the worst portion of the province of Oran, I thought the Roman historians had dealt in hyperbole when they called Africa the granary of Rome. But since I have penetrated into the country in almost every direction, my convictions have changed, and I have ascertained that Algeria produces already a great deal of corn, and an immense quantity of cattle, and that it is capable of producing much more, and also of adding several other sources of wealth, such as silk and oil." . . . . "There are vast surfaces cultivated by the Arabs, without having received any kind of manure, and yet they produce an average per hectare (2½ acres English) of from 25 to 30 hectolitres (from 70 to 85 bushels English) in wheat, and from 40 to 50 in barley." One bushel of grain generally yields from 8 to 12; and formerly English merchants used to ship from the port of Oran from 7,000 to 8,000 tons of wheat and barley annually. [Rozet.] The agricultural art of the natives is primitive and simple. The plough is a simple beam, with a coulter attached to a cross stick, which is tied to the beam. [Wilde.] Harvest usually commences at the end of May, and the stubble is burnt before the autumnal rains set in. Thrashing is performed by the tread of horses, and the only manure employed is that occasioned by the depasturing of cattle. [Smyth.] The towns and villages are encompassed with gardens decked with the richest floral productions, amongst which the rose reigns, paramount; and with orchards, in which many European fruits are found in the greatest perfection; but steppes, destitute of trees, and scantily clothed with vegetation, present themselves at frequent intervals even on the northern side of the Little Atlas. The olive-tree, which, with the pomegranate, wild | lapidaries, and jewellers. The Kabyles are at once orange, palm, and myrtle, are found in great abundance, intermingling with the brushwood which to a great extent covers the uncultivated districts in the environs of Algiers, attains very large dimensions; and the orange, lime, and citron, here rival our largest fruit-trees; but the fruit, from want of culture, is generally meagre. The dates are likewise inferior to those of the valleys of the Atlas. The vine is extensively cultivated, and the mulberry is common. The culture of cotton and of the sugar-cane is now nearly abandoned. The hedges consist of agaves and nopals, from which the inhabitants derive a material which they manufacture into cloth. The mountain-acclivities afford excellent forage. The ranges of the Little Atlas, especially those of the north, are covered with forests, consisting of the Aleppo pine, oaks, cypresses, &c. To the S of the Atlas, vegetation assumes a dwindled stunted character, and gradually disappears as we approach the arid Desert. But the date-palm arrives at its greatest perfection in the vicinity of the Desert, where it forms almost the sole vegetable production which can be made available to man's wants.—In 1832 the French government established a nursery-garden in the prov. of Algiers of 55 acres; and another now exists in the prov. of Constantine. Minerals.] The principal mineral products are iron, lead, and copper. Fossil salt is obtained in the mountains in both the eastern and the western provinces; and nitre is abundantly procured. Diamonds are reported to have been found in the neighbourhood of Constantine. The copper mines of Mouzaia were discovered by the French engineer officers, when surveying the country. The ore is broken with hammers into small pieces, and sorted according to quality: all fragments, containing a large proportion of earthy matter being rejected, as not of sufficient value to pay the expense of transport and smelting. The picked ore is then carried by mules and asses to Blidah; and from thence to Algiers, where it is shipped to France to be smelted. The ore is remarkably rich, some specimens possessing as much as 34 per cent. of copper, and the average yield of the ore imported into France is about 20 per cent. The southern slope of the mountain seems to be one immense mass of minerals; antimony is abundantly disseminated with the copper; lead has been found in small quantities, and traces of silver discovered; but the ore that exists in the greatest abundance is iron, which, from the absence of coal, is useless; neither is there in this part of the Atlas sufficient wood to supply charcoal for a furnace at a reasonable cost. Manufactures and Commerce.] The native manufactures of this country are few, and until the arrival of the French were chiefly conducted by Spaniards. They mainly consisted of silk, linen, cotton and wool stuffs, and Marocco leather. Silk is manufactured into velvets and taffeties. Coarse linen is made in every part of the country. Woollen stuffs, used for the long-hooded cloaks and for tunics, are woven by the Arab women. The carpet-stuffs are greatly inferior in quality to those of Turkey. The Algerine vessels were usually constructed of those captured from other Powers, which they took to pieces and rebuilt according to their own model. The various handicrafts were for the most part divided among several classes, who possessed in some degree the exclusive privilege of exercising them. Thus the Mozalites were the butchers, millers, and bakers; the Biskeri, porters, and guards in the ships; the Negroes were porters, assistants to masons, and white-washed the houses; the Kabyles furnished day-labourers and gardeners; the Moors were tailors, embroiderers, weavers, and dyers; and the Jews, goldsmiths, the most warlike and the most skilful and industrious race of people in A. From the mines of their mountains they obtain lead, copper, and iron. With the lead, they make bullets; with the copper, various kinds of ornaments; with the iron, gun-barrels, agricultural instruments, and various utensils. They know how to convert iron into steel, and work gold and silver. They also manufacture powder for their own use; and from olive-oil and potash make a kind of black soap, which they bring for sale to the fairs held in the Metidja. An official report on the French establishments in A. represents industry and colonization as making a more favourable progress in the country than is generally understood. In the first rank of industrial enterprises which present the most favourable chances of extensive development, is placed that of mining. The iron and copper mines of the Mouzaïa, grants of which were made in 1844, had already, it is said, begun to realize the expectations entertained. Towards the close of 1845, the government had made four new grants of iron-mines in the neighbourhood of Bona, which possessed the advantage of being situate near the extensive forests between Bona and Philippeville. Some rather important steam-engine works had been established in lage not provided with them. Essays in manufactures of various kinds were daily made, and the results had been so satisfactory that they were constantly extending. The report proceeds to notice the establishment of chambers of commerce, authorized brokers, and other appurtenant offices of industry and commerce, in all the leading places. The table of produce and merchandise brought by the Arabs to the markets of the different provinces of Algeria in 1845 states the following quantities: 184,532 oxen, 443,724 sheep, 28,336 horses, 16,802 mules, 20,417 asses, 562,328 fowls, 203,985 hectolitres of wheat, 148,259 of barley, 17,615 of vegetables, 86,898 loads of forage, 365,264 loads of wood, 160,661 loads of coal, &c. The number of Arabs who had frequented the markets in 1845 was 1,781,864, being 292,582 more than 1844. This number, of course, would comprise, to a great extent, the repeated visits of the same individuals. The supplies of the markets were yearly more abundant and on the increase. The arrivals of wool had increased by one-third in several of the markets: the total quantity was 942,000 kilogrammes. The principal bases upon which the commerce of the regency is founded consist in the resources and wants, and consequently mutual dependence of its different districts. It is divided into three zones nearly parallel to the coast. The first, bathed by the sea, and presenting a coast accessible at numerous points, receives direct the imports of European industry, and transmits them by the commerce of barter to the interior of Africa. The second comprehends the plateaus of the Atlas and the plains of the interior, the produce of whose fertility can only find an adequate market at a distance. The third is a dry, sandy, arid region extending to the banks of the Niger, rich in precious productions, but wanting in all the necessaries of life. Previous to the conquest of A. by the French, the rugs, copper, skins, ostrich-feathers, tobacco, and dates. The exportations in 1844 principally consisted of rough coral valued at 1,257,000 f.; animal-skins, at 770,192 f.; wheat, at 347,220 f.; olive-oil, at 332,820 f.; wool (unmanufactured), at 135,707 f.; unworked wax, at 88,830 f.; leeches, at 65,397 f.; barks for tanning purposes, at 51,902 f.; bones, horns, &c., at 45,203 f.; and dress feathers, at 37,165 f. The trade in wool, oil, ostrich-feathers, and tobacco, was on the increase. The places to which these articles had been exported ranked in amount as follows: France, 2,513,177 f.; Spain, 2,290,682 f.; and England, 1,047,496 f.
