Among the principal items for the last-mentioned period were: - manufactured cottons, for nearly 12,000,000 f.; grain and flour, 15,000,000 f.; woollens, 6,500,000 f.; lime, 6,500,000 f.; manufactured silks, 3,200,000 f.; refined sugar, 2,900,000 f.; timber, 2,500,000 f.; and iron and copper, 1,500,000 f. The articles which find the readiest sale among the natives are calicoes of all descriptions, ordinary manufactured stuffs, common cloths, muslins, turbans, silken and woollen girdles, cotton thread, coral for chaplets and necklaces, antimony for dyeing the eyebrows, English dyewoods, spices, spectacles, ironmongery, arms, carbines, tea, mirrors, glass wareincluding beads and rings, -- and Spanish piastres, the latter exchangeable in Sudan for their weight in gold. The description of manufactures in use among the Arabs forms a branch of trade of growing importance, and the natives are stated to be particularly attached to the woollen as well as the cotton manufactures of Europe. The consumption of imports by the Arabs is very considerable, and is set down at 12,000,000 f. for Algeria alone. In 1845 the cities of Algeria took of the imports the following proportions:-Algiers, 58 per cent.; Oran, 16 per cent.; Philippeville, 10 per cent.; Bona, 5 per cent.; Mersel-Kebir, 4 per cent.; Tenes, 1.5 per cent.; Mostaganem, 1.2 per cent.; Bugia, 0.4 per cent.; Shershel, 0.3 per cent.; Jijeli, 0.3 per cent.; and Dellys, 0.1 per cent. England for years past, according to the French journalists, had been in possession of the markets of the Barbary states, but the recent acquisition of Algeria by France had already interfered with our supremacy. Our imports, which in 1843 had been valued at 10,128,690 f., had decreased in 1844 to 5,881,376 f. This diminution, it seems, occurred principally upon cotton-manufactures, and grain from exports consisted of wheat, olive-oil, silk, woollen | the ports of Gibraltar and Malta, in consequence of an order of the French government of the 16th of December, 1843. In the year 1843, when English imports acquired their maximum amount, they stood thus:- Total, It is stated in the Algerian papers that the amount | of customs' duties received during the year 1846 amounted to 3,306,551 f., against 1,740,997 f. in the previous twelve months. This general increase of 1,565,554 f. is mentioned in these journals as showing the progress making in the trade of the colony. In the year 1845 the receipts on imports amounted to 1,103,483 f., and in 1846 to 2,417,151 f.; and the receipts on exports in the former period amounted to 9,260 f. against 8,769 f. in the latter period. The navigation dues in 1845 were 616,068 f., and in 1846, 866,477 f. These returns, it should be understood, represent the traffic of the three provinces of Algiers, Oran, and Constantine. In the case of the import trade, nearly the whole of it is carried on by French vessels. It is also the same with the export-trade, foreign ships figuring for very insignificant sums. Port-dues on foreign vessels are very high; and the duty on English goods in particular in some instances exceeds 16 per cent.—There are few roads in this country; nor can they be multiplied at pleasure. The commercial routes at least depend upon the springs of water, the grass which may be found along their line, and the practicability of the mountainpasses. Some of the new roads about Algiers are admirably constructed, particularly those leading towards Dowera and Bufario, which are made on the English Macadamized plan. Population.] We are not in possession of any exact census of the population of this country. Mr. Campbell, in his 'Letters from the South,' says: "Hamdan, a living Moorish author, whose work on Algiers has been translated into French, begins his book with a bold assertion at the first sentence, that the population amounts to 10,000,000. This conjecture is rather too gay, as it would imply this savage country to be nearly as thickly peopled as England. Shaler thinks that they scarcely exceed 1,000,000; others compute them at 2,000,000; and though I confess that I am only guessing through the guesses of others, I can scarcely suppose the whole population to exceed the latter amount. Dr. Shaw says, that according to the most exact observations which he could make himself, or receive from others, the length of the kingdom from Twunt on the E to the river Zaine on the W, may be a little more or less than 480 m. But here Dr. Shaw certainly means length as you would measure it on the globe, without including the undulations of the coast; for all the ship-masters with whom I have spoken describe the voyage between Bona and Oran as between 500 and 600 m. in length. The breadth of the kingdom is very unequal; in one part it exhibits only 40 m. between the Mediterranean on the N, and the Zahara or Desert on the S; but to the eastward of Algiers it is very considerable, and Dr. Shaw thinks that at a medium the extent of what the Arabs call tellie—meaning, that is, the land proper for tillage,—may be called 60 m. Now, if we multiply say 500 m. in length by 60 for breadth, the result will be 30,000 sq. m.; the allowance of 100 heads to a mile would make out the population to be 3,000,000; but for a people half-migratory this allowance is too large, and the whole regency does not probably contain above half that number." Shaler, who was American consul-general at Algiers for ten years, estimates the pop. at about 1,000,000, or 33 persons to a sq. m., taking the superficies at 30,000 sq. m. In the Penny Cyclopedia it is estimated at 2,000,000; and in Balbi's scale at 1,500,000. The 'Moniteur Algérien' publishes returns of the population of Algeria, from which it appears that the Europeans settled in that country, on the 31st of December, 1843, amounted to 59,186, viz., in the province of Algiers, 39,966; in that of Constantine, 9,135; and in that of Oran, 10,085. Of these 28,163 The Mozabis dwell in a distinct region in the south, were French, 5,306 English and Anglo-Maltese, 17,270 Spanish and Portuguese, 4,994 Italians, 2,797 Germans, Swiss, and Belgians, 147 Russians, Poles, and Greeks. Total, 59,186-25,393 men, 14,585 women, and 18,708 children. The native population, residing in the cities and towns, on the 31st of December, 1843, was divided as follows:-Province of Algiers, 34,553 Mussulmans, 1,340 Negroes, 6,664 Israelites; total, 42,859. Province of Constantine, 18,781 Mussulmans, 314 Negroes, 3,687 Israelites; total, 22,782. Province of Oran, 9,766 Mussulmans, 402 Negroes, 6,614 Israelites; total, 16,782. In all, 63,102 Mussulmans, 2,356 Negroes, 16,965 Israelites; total general, 82,423. The European population of the colony amounted, on the 1st of July, 1847, to 112,924 inhabitants—namely, 43,161 males, 25,605 females, and 44,158 children. That population was composed of 50,520 Frenchmen, 522 English, 140 Irish, 8,908 Anglo-Maltese, 968 Anglo-Spaniards, 33,222 Spaniards, 253 Portuguese, 8,115 Italians, 6,040 Germans, 394 Poles, 77 Russians, 59 Greeks, 3,237 Swiss, 386 Belgians and Dutch, and 83 others. In an official report made in the early part of 1847, it is stated that twelve years since there were but 11,000 colonists in Algeria: there are now 110,000. Of those 17,000 are armed as militiamen. By these means, should the entire regular army be engaged in campaign, Algiers, it is argued, can protect itself by its national guard. The report observes, that during the first years of the French occupation the colony resembled Rome before the rape of the Sabine women: there were scarcely any European women or children there. At present the European population numbers 25,000 women and 42,000 children. The report concludes by stating, that of the children of the Algerine population, 4,000 males and females, in equal proportions, are educated in schools established by the French authorities. The Negro slaves throughout the Regency—whose numbers were maintained by importation from the interior of Africa, by the inhabitants of the Desert who traffic in them—are computed at about 10,000; and the free blacks are supposed to exceed that number. The latter enjoy all the political immunities of the Moors, and were generally the bravest of the dey's soldiers. A large part of the pop. consists of Moors, a mixed race descended from the ancient Numidians or Mauritanians, the Arabs, Spaniards, and Turks, who have from time to time found their way into the country. They form the principal part of the pop. of the cultivated districts. The Europeans who have settled in A. have generally intermarried with Moorish women; but the Moors of unmixed blood are generally above the middle stature. "They have black hair; their skin is a little swarthy, but rather fair than brown; their faces are somewhat full. They are muscular, and rather corpulent; but their carriage is grave and dignified." [Rozet.] The towns are peopled by Moors, Turks, Jews, and Europeans; the Arabs occupy the low grounds; the Kabyles or Berbers inhabit the Atlas and the country of Zab; the Beni-Ammer, a nomade tribe, reside in the province of Mascara; the Coucos and Beni-Abbes dwell around Bugia; and the Henneischas on the frontiers of Tunis, and the banks of the Medjerdah. Bruce affirms that there exists, in the mountains of Auress, a tribe of a fair complexion with red hair, whom he supposes to be a remnant of the Vandal inhabitants of this country. The settled inhabitants of the Zab country are known by the appellation of Biscaris. They speak a broken dialect of Arabic; and Shaler is of opinion that they were originally of Arabian descent. The Coloris, or Kuloglus, are a mongrel race of Turkish fathers, and Moorish or Negro mothers. beyond the limits of Algerine dominion; but they hold commercial relations with Algiers; and their amin or consul was recognised in that city. - Of all the various tribes just enumerated, the Kabyles seem to be the most remarkable. They speak a language called the Showiah, which there are many reasons to believe is of great antiquity, and which Shaler thinks may have "withstood and survived the conquests of the Carthaginians, the Romans, the Vandals, and the Arabs." It is supposed to be identical with that of the Tuaricks in the interior of Libya. "They are about the middle height, thin, but strong and robust. Their complexion is brown; and sometimes almost black, with brown and glossy hair. The head of the Berber is somewhat rounder than that of the Arab, and the features shorter, but of an equally marked character." [Rozet.] The manners and customs of the Arabs of Algiers do not, according to M. Rozet's account, differ in any essential particulars from those of the Arabs of Egypt and Arabia. "The Arabs inhabiting the regency of Algiers," he says, "may be divided into two great classes: those cultivating the soil, and who occupy houses and cottages; and the Nomadic or Bedouin Arabs, who dwell in tents, and are not attached to any particular part of the country. Both belong to the same race, and speak the same language, though their manner of living is very different. The Arabs are generally tall and well-formed, their bodies fleshy, without being either fat or thin. Their hair is black, their foreheads high, their eyes quick and piercing, their mouth and nose well-made, their face oval, and their features rather long. Their skin is brown, sometimes olive; but I have seen many as black as Negroes, without, however, losing any of the other characteristics of their race. . . . The Arabs are proud and valiant; they cut off the heads of their vanquished enemies, but seldom add torture, like the Moors and the Berbers. . . The tents of the Bedouins are formed of a black and white stuff made of cotton and camels' hair, in the shape of a prism, covering a space of about 12 feet long, and six or eight wide. This serves for a whole family-often consisting of a man, three or four women, and five or six children -who sleep in it pell-mell upon mats, with the weaving loom almost always set up in the middle. The wandering tribes live under the rule of a schiek, and encamp in regular order, their tents forming a circle; and the intermediate space being allotted to their cattle. One tent in each tribe serves as a mosque. These tents are pitched so as to admit a free circulation of air, which renders them very cool in summer. When we were encamped in the peninsula of Sydi-Efroudj [Sidi Ferush] we had covered the bottom of our tents with earth all round; the consequence was, that during the heat of the day it was impossible to remain within them. In summer, the Bedouins sleep either within their tents, or in the open air around them; and the flocks and herds also remain outside, but in winter, the cows and sheep are shut up in the tents with the owners, whom they help to keep warm. There are some tents formed of several pieces of stuff joined together, and which are able to shelter vast numbers of sheep and cattle." Government.] Algiers was formerly dependent on the Porte; to whom, however, it acknowledged subjection only by sending a present to the sultan once in three years. This present was always magnificent, often amounting in value to 500,000 dollars. Its government, previous to its capture by the French, was a species of "military republic, with a chief elective for life, and upon a small scale resembling that of the Roman empire after the death of Commodus." [Shaler.] What was called the Regency was composed of the dey, the Turkish janissaries, and a council-of-state called the dowane, which generally consisted of thirty pashas. The dey was always chosen from the Turkish soldiery or corps of Janissaries, by the predominant faction in that body,-a business conducted with much tumult, and very seldom indeed without bloodshed. Tradition points out the grave of seven adventurers who were successively raised to the post and put to death on the same day! The election, however, was always recognised by the Grand Seignior, who usually created the party chosen a pasha of three tails. Of course, the possession of the country by the French has totally altered the old regime. According to the terms of the order of the 22d July, 1834, the general command and the headadministration of the French possessions in the North of Africa were confided to a governor-general under the orders of the minister-of-war. A general officer commanding the troops, a civil intendant, a general officer commanding the marine, a procureur-general, a military intendant, and a director of finances were charged, under the orders of the governor, with the different civil and military departments, and composed his council. The general economy of this organization has for the most part been continued; but, on the one hand, the heads of the various departments are authorized to correspond with the minister directly and without the intervention of the governor; on the other hand, the civil intendant has under his orders the financial department and the director of finances.—The civil administration of the French possessions in the North of Africa is intrusted, under the orders of the governor-general, to the civil intendant residing at Algiers, and to two civil subintendants resident at Oran and Bona. The civil intendant has the direction of all departments of the civil administration. He unites the different prerogatives which in France are vested in the prefects and mayors: excepting so far as relates to the acts of the civil state. The civil sub-intendants have, under the direction of the intendant, the same prerogatives, each in the city where he resides. The municipal administration of the cities of Algiers, Oran, and Bona, was at first intrusted to the intendant, the civil sub-intendants, and, under their immediate direction, to a municipal body composed of a mayor, a French assistant, a Mussulman assistant, and, according to the locality, an Israelitish assistant, and municipal counsellors. At Bugia and Mostaganem civil commissaries fulfilled provisionally the municipal functions. The municipal councils were composed of French and native notables, and their number and proportion for each locality determined by a decree of the governorgeneral. According to a royal ordonnance in 1847, the municipal body of each district is to consist of a mayor, one or more adjuncts, and a municipal council. The mayor and adjuncts of districts containing 3,000 inhabitants and upwards were to be appointed by the king; those of the other districts by the governor-general. The mayors and adjuncts are named for a period of 3 years; they must be Frenchmen, or naturalized Frenchmen, and 25 years of age. The municipal council is to consist, independently of the mayor and the adjuncts, of eight members in the districts of which the mayors are appointed by the governor, and of 12 in those where they were appointed by the king. At Algiers the municipal council is to be formed of 16 members. The municipal councillors must be Frenchmen, or naturalized Frenchmen; or, if they be foreigners, they must have obtained an authorization to exercise their civil rights in Algeria. They are eligible at 21. In districts where the natives constitute at least onetenth of the population they may be named members of the municipal council; but in no case are they to exceed one-fourth of the members of that council. The municipal councillors are to remain in office during three years, and to be chosen by the governorgeneral. The municipal councils are to meet four times annually; and each session may last 10 days. Under the old regime, the mufti of Algiers had spiritual pre-eminence over those who might be called to exercise analogous functions in the provinces, and was recognised as the superior of all the cadis. Superiority belonged to the Cadi Henefite, in conformity with the Turkish ritual; since the French occupation, it has passed to the Cadi Malekite, the northern coast of Africa having exclusively adopted the doctrine of Malek, one of the four great doctors of Islamism. At Algiers there were two cadis as well as two muftis, -one for each ritual. Besides these, special cadis were attached to the institution of the Beitel-mahl, and to the foundation of Mecca and Medina, solely to decide any disputes in which these two establishments might be interested. In each of the principal cities, according to their importance, justice was administered by one or more cadis, whose jurisdiction extended over the population generally. Every central point of population superintended by a hakim, or governor, had its cadi. A cadi was also placed by the side of the caïd, who was charged with the administration of an outhan, district, or canton. This outhan was composed of many arch (tribes), which were themselves subdivided into a certain number of douars (hamlets or unions of tents). A simple tribe had its sheikh in ordinary affairs, but when disputes of importance arose, they were referred to the cadi of the outhan. The jurisdiction of the cadis extended to all civil or criminal matters regulated by the law; that is to say, by the Koran, authentic traditions, and the commentaries sanctioned in each city by long authority; for these three elements really form the Mussulman code. The jurisdiction of the cadis was not limited to the territory for which they were instituted; a neighbouring judge could be called wherever might be the residence of the contending parties or the situation of the subject of dispute. It was only necessary that all parties should be present at the tribunal, either in person or by representative. Naval and Military force.] The naval force of the dev in 1824 consisted of 3 frigates, 2 corvettes, 2 armed brigs, 5 schooners, a polacere, and a xebec. [Shaler.] The Turks, who formed the great sinew of the Algerine army, seldom exceeded 15,000; but the corps of Chiloulis and Zouavi increased it to many more; while a call from the dey could bring in all the Arabian chiefs who happened to be well-affected at the moment towards his highness. On occasion of great emergency, it is supposed a popular dey might have brought an army of 120,000 men into the field. On the 1st January, 1845, the number of French troops in the province of Algiers was 29,193; in the province of Oran, 30,849; in that of Constantine, 18,648; in all (with the addition of the staff, &c.) 82,037 European soldiers and 8,184 natives. This enormous army was scattered in numerous detachments over the country. The hospitals were always filled, for with a force of 80,000 men, 100,915 patients entered the hospitals in consequence of sickness or the fire of the enemy; making an annual loss of 36,500 individuals; consequently during these fifteen years since the occupation, France must have lost 547,500 men. The sacrifice of money is thus calculated. Every year 5,000,000 of francs for the army, over and above the ordinary pay which the soldiers would receive if they were in France; 2,000,000 for the navy; 2,000,000 for persons employed in the different departments of civil service. viz., the administration of the interior, of finance, of the police, of rivers and forests, and of the clergy; and, finally, 1,000,000 for the secret fund, for presents and losses. All these items form a total of 10,000,000 of francs annually, which, multiplied by fifteen for the years of occupation, gives the sum of 150,000,000. This appears enormous, but is nevertheless below the mark; for the 547,500 deaths must be taken into account. Each of the men who have perished in Algeria cannot have cost less than 274 francs. It must have been necessary to prepare stations, with allowances to support them on their march from the interior to the place of embarkation; to convey them and provide for them in vessels often hired from commercial companies; to clothe, and arm them; to nurse them in the hospitals, and leave them their shirts to be buried in. Thus the whole amount is absorbed in a minimum sum assigned to each of the dead, without taking account of the living." The effective strength of the French army in Algeria, in August 1849, was about 65,000 men. Revenue.] The public revenue under the old regime is unknown. Dr. Shaw computed the whole at only 300,000 dollars,—a sum certainly below the average amount, as the French are said to have found a very considerable sum in the dey's treasury. But whatever it may have been, its only sources were the ransom of captives, the monopoly of grain, and an arbitrary impost on strangers. General Bugeaud, in his recent pamphlet on Algeria, says: "The ordinary and religious tax is the tenth of the grain and the twentieth of the cattle. If this were regularly collected—as it would be with a good government backed by a strong force—it would, I have no doubt, produce sufficient to feed the army, and provide in a great measure for its general support. I come to this conclusion on considering the enormous expenses that Abd-el-Kader has gone to to carry on the war. He not only formed and kept up an army of 12,000 to 15,000 men, but also purchased an immense number of horses, and constructed five small fortresses, a cannon-foundry, two powder manufactories, and several manufactories for arms, besides purchasing from foreign countries large quantities of muskets and ammunition, and yet he is said by the Arabs to have been still in possession of a large amount of treasure at the time when his power was overthrown. Add to this the manner in which he levied the taxes, and which is so different from our own. All the public functionaries paid themselves with their own hands, and it rarely happened that the emir received more than one-half of the amount really collected." The revenues of Algeria since it came into the possession of the French, have been gradually improving. In 1846 they were returned at 13,476,997 francs; and in 1847 at 11,708,169 francs. The following table exhibits the revenue of Algeria, from 1831 to 1841 inclusive: Amount received. Civil Population. Army. TOPOGRAPHY.] We shall now briefly sketch the topography of this region, beginning with the western province of Mascara or Oran. Shaw divides the regency into three provinces: viz., Constantine on the E; Tlemsen or Mascara on the W; and Titteri, in which he includes the city of Algiers. It is now divided into four provinces: on the N, Algiers; on the S, Titteri; on the E, Constantine; on the W, Oran. Province of Oran or Mascara.] The maritime city of Oran, in N lat. 35° 44', and 66 leagues from Algiers, stands on two small elongated plateaus which are separated by a steep valley forming the channel of a considerable stream running from W to E. This stream, which rises in a branch of the mountains of Ramra on the SW of the town, is conducted into the valley by a subterranean aqueduct, and forms a basin on the E of the town. Oran was fortified by the Spaniards, who relinquished it to the dey of Algiers in 1791. A road from Oran, which crosses the village of Kerguenta, runs E to Arzew and Mostaganem. That from Mascara and Algiers passes through the same village, and the plateau which forms the cemetery of Oran; and another from Tlemsen runs past the village of Raz-el-Ain, in a SE direction, along the mountains of Akebet Arun. The fort of Mirs-el-Kebir is 3 m. NW of Oran; Arzew, in N lat. 35° 52', is 23 m. NE of Oran; and Mostaganem is 19 m. E of Arzew. About fifty years since, the territories of Mostaganem, Metamore, and Mazagran or Missigran, between the estuaries of the Shelif and the Magta, contained a pop. of 25,000. In 1830 the pop. of that district was reduced to a half of that number; but until 1833, the environs of Mostaganem continued to present a flourishing aspect. After the French conquest, these appearances of prosperity disappeared. Abd-el-Kader carried away the old population of Mazagran to Tekedempt, and substituted for it refugees of the tribes of Bethowa, Mekalia, Chourfa, and Borgia. The latter tribe inhabited the territory of El-Borg until 1836, when it was transferred to the Mina near Akermas.—The town of Mascara, situated on the southern side of the hills which close the plain of Eghrès on the N, stands on two eminences, separated by a ravine through which a stream constantly flows.—Tlemsen is built on a declivity about 4 m. from the sea. Several streams descend from the mountain-ranges which rise behind the town; and on the S lies a well-watered valley, whose luxuriance presents a striking contrast to the sterile aspect of the mountain-piles in the distance. At the period at which Oran fell into the hands of the Spaniards, the kingdom of Tlemsen comprised all the valley of the Shelif; and in the 3d. cent. it held in dependance the prov. of Bugia. The town itself was celebrated for the greatness of its public establishments, and for the intelligence and wealth of its merchants; and was much frequented by the Genoese and Venetians.—At the extremity of the beautiful plain of Tlemsen, on the confines of Marocco, is the Wad-Zeitun, or 'River of olive-trees;' further on is the Suf-Neurours; and beyond that, the Wad-Tafna, the largest river in the district. The country which lies between the Tafna and Marocco is sandy, sterile, and uncultivated. The Wad-el-Melch, a tributary of the Tafna, flows at the foot of the range of the Trara, which extends a distance from N to S of about 50 m. Province of Constantine.] This province, which originally belonged to Tunis, is better known, and has been more frequently described, than that of Oran. The extensive territory which it comprehends was geographically divided into four parts. The Chark or East, embraced the country between Constantine and the frontier of Tunis; the Gharb or West, that between Constantine and the Biban range; the Kiblah or South, comprised the country from Constantine to the Great desert, including the Sahara; and the Dharah or North, embraced the coast, commonly called Sahel, from Bona to Bugia. The fertility of the soil of this prov. is very great. Maize and millet are largely produced; cotton was formerly grown; the soil is favourable to the mulberry-tree; and tobacco, the vine, and the olive are cultivated with success. The pop. of this province is divided into three distinct races: the Arabs inhabiting the southern district; the Chaouïa in the central district; and the Kabyles in the northern part along the sea-coast. The Arabs regard the other races with contempt. The Chaouïa, who represent the vanquished aborigines of this province, are agriculturists, and devote themselves chiefly to the culture of grain. They speak a different language from the Arabs, and make an imperfect profession of Mahommedanism. The Kabyles are an independant race, sedentary, and very industrious in their habits. They manufacture iron and powder. These races are subdivided into tribes, of which the most celebrated are those of the Hauenchah of Arab origin, on the frontier of Tunis; the Haractah and the Nememchah, to the S of the town of Tibessah; the Zeboul, the aborigines of the environs of Msilah, a military tribe who established themselves near Constantine; the Barauiah, a word signifying 'stranger,' and indicating the more recent settlement of this tribe in the district adjacent to the Zemoul; the Aa'mer Cheragah, in the SW of Constantine, covering the beautiful plain of Mihris, and the immense plateau known by the name of Sera des Aa'mer; on the W of Constantine, the Ouled Abd-el-Nour, a numerous tribe of the Chaouïa race; the Telagmah of the same race in the SW of Constantine; the Aa'mer Gherabah, whose vast territory is bounded on the N by the Jebel Maghris and the Sahel Babour; the Ouled Mokran of Arab origin, who form the aristocracy of the plain of the Medjanah. Besides these larger tribes there were in different parts of the province many smaller, who were not governed by caïds, and who were named the Beylik, because they paid tribute direct to the pasha.—The Sahel—signifying in Arabic 'sea-coast'-lying between Bona and Bugia, is bounded on the S by the chain of mountains which extends from Raz-el-Akbah to the Jebel Maghris, and is divided into several parts, named Sahel de Skiddah, in which is included the territory of Storah, Sahel de Collo, Sahel de Jijely, Sahel Babour, Sahel de Bugia. The French had established a commercial intercourse with this district of the regency, and had projected the formation of a military port as early as the year 1664; but they evacuated it shortly after that period. It has been reoccupied by them since the 13th of May, 1839.—The towns of Collo and Jijely, and the tribes inhabiting the district of the Sahel, did not acknowledge the authority of the pasha.—Bugia is built on the site of the ancient Saldae, one of the principal towns of Cæsarian Mauritania, and the capital of the Vandal empire. The Saracens took possession of it in 662; and it prospered greatly under the princes of the Arab family of Beni- Hamad, who kept it from 991 to 1151, when Abdel Moumen, sultan of Marocco, chief of the Almohades, made a conquest of the province of Bugia. In 1240 it passed from the sovereignty of Tlemsen to that of Tunis; in 1510 was taken by Peter of Navarre; and in 1555 fell into the hands of the Turks, and was by them placed under the administration of a special governor, who bore the title of caid, and exercised absolute power of life and death over its inhabitants and the adjacent tribes of Kabyles .- The mountains which surround Bugia are very populous. Within a compass of 12 leagues, no fewer than 30 tribes have been enumerated here. The caïd of Bugia does not levy imposts beyond the city, except on the neighbouring tribe of the Mozaia.—The town of Bona—in Arabic Blaid-el-Aneb-is situated in N lat. 36° 54', and E long. 7° 46', near the site of the ancient Hippona. It was destroyed in 1832, but has been rebuilt and continues to increase slowly, although the new town of Philippeville has drawn off much of the trade of Constantine from Bona, particularly that of supplying the troops. The present pop. amounts to some 8,000, of whom about a third are natives, and 800 or 1,000 Maltese. Added to this civil pop. there are about 4,000 troops. The tribe of the Beni-Urdjin continue to occupy and enrich themselves with the produce of the fertile district near this town between the Seybus and Mafrag, on which they were established when they fled from the persecution of Ahmed Bey.—The little town of Calle or La Cala, which lies ENE of Constantine, was to a great extent destroyed by fire in 1827. It was taken possession of by the French in 1836, but their first settlements on this coast were cotemporary with those of the Turks. In 1520, some French merchants entered into a treaty with the Mazoule tribes for the coral-fishery between Tabarca and Bona. Selim II., under Charles IX., conceded to France the commerce of Malfacarel, Calle, Collo, Capa Rosa and Bona; and the concession was confirmed in 1624 by Amurath IV. The Bastion de France, 12 m. to the W of La Calle, completed in 1560, was abandoned in 1690 on account of its offering no shelter for shipping.—To the number of coast towns belonging to this province has recently been added the new port of Philippeville established in connexion with Constantine. All communication between Algiers and Constantine is now carried on by sea by the ports of Bona and Philippeville. A connexion has likewise been recently formed between Collo and the latter port.—The town of Constantine, situated in N lat. 36° 24', 30 leagues S of Bona, stands on the summit of a hill, bathed on almost all sides by the Wad-el-Kebir, which issues from a subterranean source, and forms a remarkable cascade in the upper part of the town, 600 ft. above the level of the plain. It is the principal market for agricultural produce, being resorted to by even the most distant tribes in preference to Telagmah, Ouled-Abd-el-Nour, Ferdjiouah, or Setif.—Setif, the ancient Sitifis Colonia, is situated in an extensive and fertile plain, watered by the Wad-Bon-Selam. It was one of the most considerable settlements founded by the Romans in Africa, and gave its name to a division of Mauritania. Its general prosperity, and in particular its cotton plantations, are frequently noticed by Arab historians. The attention of the French being attracted to Setif by the fertility of the soil and the central position of the town, they made it first a small military station, and subsequently made it the cap. of a subdivision with a military corps of 2,600 men. A connexion is likely to be formed between this town and the port of Bugia. The neighbouring tribes are generally pacific in their dispositions, and devoted to agriculture. The roads between Setif and Constantine are mere pathways formed by the Arab muleteers; one passes through the mountainous districts of Milah, Maallah, and Djimilah, and another through the territory of the Telagmah, the Ouled-Abd-el-Nour, and the Eulmah-de-Bazr. Guelma or Ghelma, lies near the S bank of the Upper Seibus, at the foot of the high mountain of Maouna. It is built of materials taken from the ruins of the ancient Calama mentioned by St. Augustine; but the site which it occupies is not that of the Roman city. Several roads diverged from Calama; two ran in the direction of Hippone along the banks of the Seibus; another to Constantine, passing to the N of Mount Maouna across the Wad- Cherff, and rising gradually to the summit of the Amouna; and at least two more ran towards the S, probably to Zama and Tiffech, and thence in various ramifications across their beautiful plains.—The town of Msilah, situated in N lat. 35° 421, is divided into three parts, of which the most considerable lies on the left bank, and the two others on the right of the Wad-Ksab. The gardens occupy a surface three | pitous on all sides except the S, skirted by the tributimes greater than the extent of the town. The materials of which Msilah is built belonged to a Roman city, the ancient Siulia, the Bechilga of the Arabs, now in ruins, which stood further to the E. The French established themselves in this city in June 1841.—SSW of Msilah, at the foot of the Jebel- Salah, is the town of Bou-Saadah, containing a pop. of about 2,000, and surrounded with gardens of greater extent and beauty than those of the lastmentioned city.—Half-way between these towns— which are separated by a distance of about 50 m.—is a Roman station, now called El-Beniau, in ruins. The Arabs place the extremity of the Sahara to the S of Msilah, bounding it for a distance by the chain of the Saadah. The chief of the Sahara possessed the title of Sheikh-el-Arab. His authority extended in the N from the mountains of Auress and Belezmah, which separate the Sahara from the Tell, or the uncultivated plain from the hilly region, to the district of Msilah; on the S to the district of Souf, and from Tuggurt, which formed the boundary of the Beled-el-Djerid of Tunis on the E to the territory of the town of Aghouath on the W. This immense territory is occupied by two distinct classes of pop.— the wandering Arabs, who pass the winter in the Sahara, and the inhabitants of the small towns of the oases. Biskarah, the capital, stands on the eastern side of a circle of forty of these towns. It is under the government of a caid, whose jurisdiction extends over the territory of the Zaab. The Zaab of Tuggurt contains 14 small towns, which are not populous. The remote position of the sheikh of Tuggurt renders him nearly independent.—The country of Souf is divided into seven large tribes. Its towns, consisting of a few cottages, are very poor, but they are surrounded by beautiful and luxuriant gardens. The old and important commercial connexions which subsist between Constantine and the Desert, derive their permanence from the dependence of the districts of Tuggurt and of Zaab of Biskarah upon that city for even the most necessary means of subsistence. Province of Algiers.] The position of the city of A. on the great maritime and military depot of the regency,-the convergency towards its walls of the lower ranges of the Atlas,—the vicinity of the Metidja and of the valley of the Shelif, of which the Metidja may be considered an extension,—naturally assign to Algiers the pre-eminence which it enjoys as capital of the regency.—The plain of Metidja, running from NE to SW, is from 50 to 55 m. in length, and from 18 to 21 m. in breadth. It is well-cultivated near the mountains, but marshy and thickly inhabited in the lower parts. Buffarik, a fortified town, occupies the site of a famous market-place in the Metidja; it was the first, and is destined to become the central establishment of the French in the plain. It is situated between Doueira and Blidah, on the principal road from Algiers, and at the point of its divergence to Oran on the W and Blidah on the SE.—Belida or Blidah is beautifully situated near the base of the Little Atlas, 36 m. SW of Algiers, at the opening of a deep valley. It contained 7,000 inhabitants previous to the earthquake in 1825, which destroyed a large portion of the town. Its environs are well-cultivated. The neighbouring mountains are inhabited by the tribes of the Beni-Meissera on the E, and those of the Beni-Sala and Beni-Messous on the W. The latter tribe extends to the valley of the Shefah, beyond which the territory of the Mouzaia commences. From Blidah the road runs westerly along the banks of the Wad-Kebir, crosses the chain of the Little Atlas, and enters the great valley in which Mediah is situated, by the plateau of Nador. -This town stands on an eminence which is precitaries of the Shelif and the upper part of the basin of the Wad-Djir, and surmounted by a Roman fortress, of which some of the ramparts still exist. The town is about 3,606 ft. above the level of the sea, and the extremes of its summer and winter temperature are considerable. The vine is the principal article of culture in the environs; mulberry, pear, and poplar trees are the most common. When the French formed an establishment in this town in 1840, it was nearly a desert, but it now promises to become an important medium of communication between the Sahara, Beni-M'zab, and Algiers. A Roman road runs from Mediah first S and then E, round the Jurjura and Biban to Constantine; another joins Mediah and Milianah.—The latter town, of which the French took possession on the 8th of June, 1840, is situated in a mountainous district, about 2,952 ft. above the level of the sea, and 5 m. from the plain of the Shelif. The pop., at the period of its first occupation by the French, was 7,000 or 8,000, consisting of the inimical Arab tribes of the Righa, the Beni - Menasser, Reyra, Lachem, Beni - Zug - Zug, &c., and Moors or Koulouglis. The environs are very fertile, and are watered by streams proceeding from the unfailing sources of the Zakkar on the NW. The temperature is moderate but variable; the heat is less than at Algiers, Bona, or Oran; the winds are likewise very variable, changing frequently in the course of a day; the climate, however, is not insalubrious. There are traces of a Roman road on the E of the town. The chief advantages which attach importance to Milianah are the command it possesses of the upper part of the Shelif and of the rich tribes of the valley of that river, and its being placed on the line of communication between Algiers and the principal towns of the province of Oran.—Shershel, a maritime town in the prov. of Algiers, 60 m. W of the capital, stands on the site of the Roman city Julius Casarea, on the southern acclivity of a range of hills which rise 328 ft. above sea-level, in a salubrious, fertile district, sheltered on the S by the chain of the Zakkar. The environs are woody, well-cultivated, and watered by several streams, of which the principal and unfailing are the Wad-Bellaa and the Wad-el-Hachem. Previous to the taking of Shershel by the French in 1840, it conducted an active trade with Algiers in fruits, and timber, and faggots. This town has the advantage of commanding the eastern side of the Metidja, from which it is separated only by a low range of hills, and a district of about 15 m. across, known by the name of the Sahel des Beni-Menad.—Dellys, in Arabic Tedles, is another maritime town 60 m. to the E of Algiers. When the two Barbarossas divided the regency in 1517, Hayradin established his residence in Dellys. Nicolas of Nicolai, who visited it in 1551, and more recently Gramaye, have extolled the industry and virtue of its inhabitants. Its pop. has since been reduced by the oppression of the Turks to 600. History.] This country was, in the earliest period of authentic history, divided between two nations,—the Massyli, on the side of Africa Proper, the subjects of Masinissa—and the Masswsyli towards Mauritania, under the dominion of Syphax, a Getulian prince. On the defeat of Juba by Cæsar, the territory, once the land of heroic deeds, and the domain of powerful kings, was reduced to a Roman province, embracing Numidia, Mauritania Sitifensis, and Mauritania Casariensis. These proud conquerors of the world were driven from their African possessions by the Vandals; and these again were expelled by Belisarius, under Justinian, about the middle of the 6th cent. A hundred years afterward, another revolution was effected by the Saracens; and the subsequent history of this country, down to the 16th cent., is blended with that of Marocco. In the beginning of the 16th cent., the Spaniards, ever inveterate enemies of the Moors, encouraged by the prevalence of civil commotions, invaded Algiers. On the 3d of September, 1505, a Spanish fleet set sail from Malaga, and appeared on the 11th in the roadstead of Mers-el-Kebir, in the bay of Oran. On the 23d of October, that town capitulated to the Spanish, one of whose generals, Don Gonsalvo de Ayora, in reporting the success of the expedition to its great founder, Cardinal Ximenes, wrote, "Africa is now half-conquered." It was soon found necessary, however, to reduce the neighbouring city of Oran, which was effected on the 14th of May, 1509, under the personal observation of Cardinal Ximenes. Bugia, once one of the most important towns of the Mediterranean, and still more populous and wealthy than Oran, was captured soon after, and on the 31st of January, 1510, the sultan of Algiers recognised the sovereignty of Spain. Instead, however, of occupying the city, the count of Navarre was contented to build, at an enormous cost, a fortress on the rocky islet opposite the town, to keep the Algerines in awe and insure payment of their tribute. In 1516, the weak sultan Selim Ben Tehaim summoned Arondj Barbarossa, the zealous defender of Islamism, to his aid. Barbarossa speedily signalized his new career, by causing Selim to be murdered, and himself elected in his stead. By repeated acts of treachery, or of courage, Barbarossa's dominions were gradually extended to Tlemsen. He was succeeded by his brother, Hayradin, who, finding himself harassed by the Arabs and Moors on one side, and by the Spaniards on the other, made his dominions a province of the Turkish empire by placing them under the protection of the Porte, and further secured his power in Algiers by fortifying the bay in a strong manner. He is said to have employed 30,000 Christian slaves for three years in building the mole. The depredations committed by the Algerines upon the mercantile marine of Europe now became so alarming, that Pope Paul made use of his influence with Charles V. to prevail on him to chastise these piratical infidels; and issued a bull offering absolution of sins and the crown of martyrdom to such as should either be killed or made prisoners in this contest. Charles sailed against the Algerines, with 120 ships, and 20 galleys, having on board 30,000 chosen men. The knights of Malta also gladly embraced the opportunity of facing their hereditary enemy, and joined him with many of their best troops. This formidable armament landed without opposition, and proceeded to attack Algiers, when a storm, which happened on the 28th of October, 1541, in one night sunk 86 ships and 15 galleys with all their crews. Charles witnessed this disaster from his camp in silent agony, and immediately retired by land to Metafuz, whence he returned with his remaining troops to Carthagena. In 1585, we first find the Algerines passing the straits of Gibraltar; and about this time they obtained from the Turkish government the power of choosing their own dey, and of making laws for their own government. This bestowed upon the country new vigour, and allured thither the numerous Moors who had been expelled from Spain in 1609. Many of these Moors, being excellent seamen, proved a valuable acquisition to a maritime state; and the Algerine fleet thus augmented, became formidable, not only to the Spaniards and Portuguese with whom they were openly at war, but even to the French and English with whom they professed to be at peace. In 1617, the French sent against them Beaulieu, with 50 sail. He defeated the fleet of the Algerines, but captured only two of their vessels. The English, instigated, no doubt, by the insults which they continually received from Moorish cruisers, in 1620 sent a squadron against Algiers, under the command of Sir Robert Mansell, who returned without being able to effect the purpose of his equipment. The depredations committed by Algerine corsairs upon the Italian coasts, at length provoked the Venetians to take vengeance upon a power which had become so troublesome. Capello was despatched with a commission to attack the Algerines wherever he could find them. He succeeded in taking 16 of their galleys; but they soon recovered their former vigour, and within two years appeared again at sea with a fleet of 65 ships. Louis fitted out a fleet under the Marquis Du Quesne, which, in 1681, attacked the Algerine fleet near the island of Scios, and destroyed 14 of their vessels. In 1682, he bombarded Algiers itself, and laid it almost entirely in ruins. The Algerines, by way of reprisal, sent a fleet to the coast of Provence, where they made dreadful ravages: whereupon Du Quesne returned to Algiers in 1683, and bombarded the city so successfully that the dey desired to capitulate, when Mezomorte, the Algerine admiral, who was to have been delivered as an hostage for the performance of the treaty, made such use of his influence that he procured the murder of the dey, and was himself invested with the dignity. This interruption of the capitulation led to a more furious attack on the part of the French; and Mezomorte, thinking only of revenge, ordered every French prisoner to be put to death, and the French consul to be fixed to the mouth of a mortar, and shot off against the bombarding fleet. Du Quesne thereupon redoubled his efforts, and laid the lower town and two-thirds of the upper in ruins; and the Algerines, convinced that further resistance must terminate in their destruction, despatched an embassy to Louis to beg peace, which was granted. The English also, in 1686, effected a treaty with the Algerines on reasonable terms, which was successively renewed by James II. William and George II. These treaties formed the basis of all the intercourse betwixt Great Britain and the Algerine regency, until the expedition of Lord Exmouth. The Spaniards, notwithstanding their partial occupancy of the country, appear to have allowed the Turkish power to extend itself without opposition over the Moghreb territory. Hassan, the son of Hayradin, had succeeded in concentrating the authority of several caïds in the hands of a bey whose residence was fixed at Mazonna, between Mostaganem and Tenes, within a league of the Shelif; and in 1622 Oran itself was attacked, though unsuccessfully, by Bey Chaban. But it was not until 1708 that the Turks deter mined on making one vigorous effort to drive the Christians of Oran beyond the sea. A French prince had recently succeeded to the throne of Spain on the death of the last descendant of Charles V.; but this succession kindled at the same time a civil and a foreign war. Spain, divided, weakened, preoccupied with nearer interests, gave but little attention to her African possessions, and the Turks took advantage of her distress. Mustapha-bou-Shelagham, then bey of Oran, who had recently transported the seat of the beylik to Mascara, received from the dey of Algiers an order to assemble all his troops, and in all haste lay siege to Oran. Philip V. had succeeded in collecting a sufficient number of boats, troops, and ammunition, to oppose the enemy: unfortunately however, the treachery of the Count de la Vera Cruz betrayed the forces destined for the defence of Oran into the hands of the Archduke Charles. In the absence of the assistance they were expecting, the garrison of Oran offered little or no resistance; they capitulated, and Mustapha-bou-Shelagham took possession of the town in the name of the dey of Algiers: Oran thus became the seat of government in the west, and the ordinary residence of the bey. No sooner did Philip V. find himself firmly seated on his throne, than he declared his intention to re-conquer Oran. The manifesto published at Seville June 7, 1732, in which Philip declares to his subjects and to all Europe his project of restoring to Spain her ancient conquest, is thoroughly imbued with a religious spirit, though it doubtless participated more in the political than the pious. The Turks had been in possession of Oran 24 years, when the Spanish government commenced preparations for an expedition with the purpose of expelling them from the territory. Nothing that might insure success was left undone. The king commanded a levy of 30,000 men to be made, intrusted the command of the several divisions of his army to his most skilful generals, and invested Count Montemart with the office of commander-in-chief. The fleet, consisting of 12 ships-of-the-line, 2 frigates, 2 oar-galleys, 12 armed long-boats, and above 500 transport-ships carrying 25,000 men, set sail from Alicant on the 15th of June, 1732, but having been driven by contrary winds to touch at Cape Palos during seven days, did not arrive before Oran until the 25th. On the following day a landing was effected without any serious opposition; and on the 30th of June, Oran and all its fortifications were abandoned. The Spaniards found in the place 146 field-pieces, a large number of mortars, and abundance of provisions. Some time after, the Turks made a fruitless attempt to recover the place. At this time were constructed Fort St. Andrew and Chateau Neuf, which defend the place: the first on the SE, the second on the E, and nearer the sea. Intercourse with the tribes becoming more difficult, the garrison was increased to 2,000 men. Nevertheless the taking of Oran did not entirely re-establish even the precarious situation the Spaniards had held in the province. The natives, cooled by a first retreat, kept still further aloof than formerly, and the history of the settlement during 60 years was merely that of an unimportant fortress or port. This possession was vegetating without prospect of advancement, when a great catastrophe gave Spain a pretext for throwing off the burden. During the night of the 9th of October a frightful earthquake was felt at Oran and in the environs. It consisted at first of several successive convulsions at short intervals, which were frequently repeated until the 22d of November. The consequence of this was the destruction of the edifices, houses, forts, and fortifications of the place, and the death of more than a third of the garrison and a considerable number of the inhabitants. The remainder of the garrison were in the most horrible distress; they were without tents, barracks, hospitals, and even medicaments for the sick and wounded, and had lost their stores and provisions. To complete their misery, the bey of Mascara, Mahommed-el-Kebir, taking advantage of the general consternation, appeared as an enemy before the fortress; and the Spanish troops, panic-struck and driven to despair, were about to engage with an army of 30,000 men. Reinforcements were, however, received from Carthagena, Majorca, and Cordova, and, by dint of energy and skill, the commander-in-chief succeeded in defending the ruins until the month of August in the following year, when the regency of Algiers entered into negotiations for a treaty of peace and commerce similar to that which had been signed in 1786. The bey of Mascara received an order to withdraw his army and suspend hostilities. This gave the Spaniards breathing time of which they took advantage to make an honourable surrender. By a convention between the government of Oran and the Bey Mahommed, it was determined that the fortifications should not be destroyed, that the town should be evacuated within a certain term, and that the Spaniards should remove their bronze cannon and military stores. The troops and Christian inhabitants were conveyed to Carthagena. Mahommed, who had remained before the walls of the town until its complete evacuation, effected his entry at the beginning of March, 1792. Thus ended, by the desertion of the last town which they had continued to keep, although of no advantage to them, the occupation of the ancient regency of Algeria by the Spanish. After 283 years, illustrated no doubt by many a glorious struggle, but employed only in the possession of the coast, they were finally induced to desert it. In 1807 the French colonies on the coast were ceded by the regency to the English; and they were not restored to France until 1816. Our establishments at Gibraltar and Port Mahon preserved our relation with Algiers more stable than any other European state; but the general peace in 1816 induced the British government to demand some permanent arrangements from the Algerines. They were required to treat the inhabitants of the Ionian islands as British subjects; peace was negotiated by our com- 1 mander in the Mediterranean for Sardinia and Naples; and the liberation of all European captives upon payment of a stipulated ransom, with the entire cessation of such a nefarious traffic in future, was imperiously demanded. Mortifying as these conditions must have been, under the terrors inspired by the presence of a British fleet, they were instantly accepted. Scarcely, however, had Lord Exmouth departed, when the treaty was violated by the massacre of a large body of Neapolitan coral-fishermen at Bona. An expedition, still more formidable than the former, was instantly placed under his lordship's command. The British fleet was joined at Gibraltar by 5 Dutch frigates, and appeared before Algiers on the 18th of August, 1816. The dey was prepared to repel force by force; but on the 27th August, the Algerine fleet was reduced to ashes,-the greater part of the batteries silenced, -one-half of the town destroyed,-and no course left to the Algerine ruler but to cast himself on the mercy of the British admiral. The terms now imposed were the instant liberation of all captives without ransom,-the repayment of all sums received as ransom-money since the commencement of the current year,and the abolition of Christian slavery for ever. The dey was also required to make a public apology to the British consul for having thrown him into prison. Lord Exmouth had the satisfaction of informing the Admiralty, on the 1st of September, that all the slaves in Algiers, amounting to 1,211—amongst whom there was not one Briton-were already embarked, with 357,000 dollars for Naples, and 25,000 for Sardinia. Notwithstanding the infliction of such signal punishment on these barbarians, Algerine corsairs continued to commit piracies upon French and Austrian vessels; and in 1827 a public insult offered by the dey to the French consul caused a sudden rupture between the two countries. For three years the French blockaded Algiers; and at last in May, 1830, an expedition was fitted out from Toulon for the purpose of bombarding the place. The total military force employed on this occasion, exclusive of the marine force, amounted to 37,577 men; and the French government professed its willingness, in the event of a dissolution of the Algerine government, "to concert with the allies of France for the purpose of deciding what should be the new order of things which might be substituted with the greatest benefit to Christendom for the system destroyed." The trenches were opened on the 29th of June, and on the 5th of July the city capitulated to the French arms under the following treaty between Count De Bourmont the general-in-chief of the French army and his highness the dey:-"The fort of the Casbah, all the other forts dependent on Algiers, and the port of this city, are to be surrendered to the French troops this morning at 10 o'clock.-The general-in-chief of the French army engages to leave his highness the dey of Algiers his liberty and the possession of all his personal property.—The dey is to be free to retire with his family and his private property to a place which he shall appoint; and as long as he remains at Algiers both he and his family shall be under the protection of the general-in-chief of the French army. A guard shall guarantee the safety of his person and his family.—The general-in-chief promises the same advantages and the same protection to all the soldiers of the militia. —The exercise of the Mahommedan religion is to remain free.— The liberty of the inhabitants of every class, their religion, property, commerce, and labour, are not to be affected. Their women shall be respected.—The general-in-chief enters into the engagement on his honour. -- The exchange of this treaty shall be made before 10 o'clock this morning; and the French troops shall then enter the Casbah, and successively all the forts both of the city and the coast." The capitulation, which destroyed the Algerine government after a duration of three centuries, gave to France no more than a single city. Excepting the space occupied by the victorious troops around the conquered capital, between the hills of Bujeria and those of Mustapha Pasha, there was no recognised dominion acquired by the new masters of Algiers. The ancient power which by a number of violent means combined a heterogeneous population being once dissolved, the ties which united to Algiers the tribes beyond its immediate territory were broken at one blow. All who were or believed themselves to be in a position to profit by the disorganization of the country began to rise; and as the hatred of the Christian name was still more lively in the country than in the cities, the dissensions which prevailed amongst themselves did not prevent the native tribes from preparing to combine and fight against foreign and infidel France. Accordingly, the French have ever experienced considerable difficulty in maintaining their footing in Africa, and they only preserve it by the constant presence of a large body of troops. For our own part, we are inclined to wish success to the efforts of France to retain her conquest. She requires some outlet for a portion of the 200,000 souls which, according to Dupin, she adds to her population every year. Now, in point of climate, soil, and situation, Algeria is one of the most desirable countries in the world; while Marseilles, or Toulon, is not above three days' sail from Algiers, and the expense of conveying an emigrant from the one country to the other need not much exceed 20s. Were the French therefore to succeed in permanently colonising this part of Africa, it might do much for the general cause of human advancement. In fifty years we might see the present miserable race of barbarians who occupy Mauritania replaced by or amalgamated with a community of one or two millions cultivating the arts and commerce, and extending the empire of knowledge and civilization and Christianity over one of the fairest tracts of this earth's surface. Let France accomplish this, and she will deserve the gratitude of the human race. The late French governor-general of Algeria, Marshal Bugeaud, L in a pamphlet on the means of preserving and rendering useful the conquest of Algeria, from experience doubtless, remarks: "With such a population there is no such thing as insinuating one's self into power;" and proceeds to argue that "they must be conquered in order to obtain possession of and colonize a portion of their territory." France then, he contends, has acted wisely, having once resolved on establishing herself in Algeria, in being also resolved to conquer it entirely. It would be difficult to preserve upon the defensive system any portion of the coast, for in that case the enemy would be left in full enjoyment of the resources of taxation and recruitment, and "would be able to keep us in a continual state of blockade, and to attack us whenever he should become strong or we should have embarrassments to contend with in Europe." The system of conquest too, the Marshal remarks, is not only less difficult than that of defence; but it also presents immense advantages for colonization. "There are a number of objects, such as cattle, horses, mules, asses, forage, vegetables, poultry, wood, charcoal, &c., with which colonists could only be supplied by sea at enormous prices. Cheap labour supposes, therefore, freedom of internal communication, which can only exist in Algeria by means of a peace always precarious, or by the military possession of the whole or nearly the whole of the country." General Bugeaud then proceeds to consider the means of effecting and securing the conquest which he recommends; and, on this point, frankly says: "I do not hesitate to assert that the same or nearly the same amount of force as will have effected the conquest will be indispensable for its maintenance. The Arabs are proud and warlike. The war of tribe against tribe is their normal state. From their infancy all the males, without exception, are trained to the use of arms and the management of horses; and they are incessantly engaged in hazardous enterprises. We cannot think of diminishing our force in presence of such a population. We should soon suffer from such an act of imprudence. The history of the Arabs shows us how prompt they are to revolt, and their antipathy for us and for our religion will last for ages." This course, the Marshal thinks, is the more obviously pointed out by the consideration that "it is the only way to prevent our occupation from becoming an onerous one to ourselves. With an army of sufficient strength, we can govern the country, collect the taxes, and promote commercial speculation. The contributions of the interior, the revenues of the customs and the octrol, and the resources which the troops will be able to create in their stations by the cultivation of a portion of the rich soil, will in a few years defray the expense of occupation." After pursuing at some length the same line of argument, the Marshal gives his opinion of the amount of permanent force necessary in Algeria, and the mode of distributing it. "We ought," says he, "to have two lines of occupation; one in the interior and parallel with the sea, the other on the coast. The stations for the first appear to me to be clearly pointed out; they are Tlemsen, Mascara, Milianah, Mediah, Sétif, Constantina, and Ghelma; on the coast, Oran, Mostaganem, Tenes, Shershel, Algiers, Philippeville, and Bona. I have not spoken of Arzew, Bugia, Jijelly, or Calle, for these localities are generally of slight importance, and I think we should evacuate Bugia and Jijelly if we are not decided upon bringing under subjection the Kabyle mountains which surround them." To these views and arguments of Marshal Bugeaud M. Blanqui is wholly opposed. He is of opinion that colonization by the French is almost hopeless in Algeria. The climate, the insalubrity proceeding from the soil, the insecurity, the ill success of every attempt hitherto, have been, he says, insurmountable obstacles. To drain the land, extirpate the race, or send two French soldiers to watch each Arab, were necessary preliminaries of colonization by France. No dependence whatever is to be placed on the natives, either the Arabs of the plain, or the Kabyles of the hills. "We have no more power over them," says M. Blanqui, "than the Americans over the savages of the Ohio." Authorities.] Shaw's Travels in Barbary.—Sketches of Algiers. By W. Shaler, American Con. Gen. at Algiers.
