ALGIERS, [ARAB. Al-Jezirah, i.e. 'the Island,'] the capital of the above state, is situated in N lat. 36° 47', E long. 3° 4' [Berard], in a cove on the W side of a bay to which it gives name, nearly opposite to Minorca, and 380 m. W of Tunis. It lies upon the side of a hill, which rises from the shore in the form of an amphitheatre; and as the houses, in general, have flat white roofs, it appears from sea "like a field covered with bleached linen," or "the top-sail of a ship," or "a chalk-mound in the midst of an amphitheatre of green mountains. A little nearer, its square towers, mosques, and minarets become distinguishable, rising above each other like a flight of steps from the water's edge to the summit of the mountain [400 ft.]. Right and left spread undulating heights, whose luxuriant verdure, interspersed with gay kiosks, forms an agreeable contrast to the tiring glare of the white houses. In the back-ground are still loftier hills, whose sterile tops give another variety to the picture. But whatever attraction the city may offer at a distance, the illusion entirely vanishes on setting foot on shore. The streets are narrow and filthy, and there are no buildings of importance, save the barracks of the Janissaries." There is one broad street running through it from E to W and containing the shops of the principal merchants, the corn and other markets: the rest of the streets, until the French got possession of the town, were so narrow, that two persons had hardly room to walk abreast. The houses also were "all alike in form and disposition, though some are better than others. They are squares or rectangles formed by four walls, which rise commonly to the height of a third floor, and are pierced by small holes to let the air pass, but scarcely ever furnished with windows." [Rozet.] The houses on opposite sides of the street moreover approached each other, and projected in proportion as they rose from the ground. The motive of this arrangement seems to have been the desire to find shelter from a burning sun. But the French have nearly rebuilt A., and the town is now well-opened and laid out .- The cassaba, or dey's palace or castle, is an octagonal fort, now a barrack, on the eastern side of the city, and elevated above it, forming the apex of its triangular outline. Here the dey kept his treasures. In the lower part of the town, there was also a palace called the Jenina. A castle called the Emperor's, outside the walls, commanded both the city and the cassaba; and was itself commanded by the upper part of Mount Bujeria. The mole, which forms the harbour, runs above 1,400 paces into the sea; and is built on a ledge of rocks within which the navy is all secured in case of attack. The labour of European engineers, since the siege by Lord Exmouth, has rendered this part of the fortifications unassailable, and leaves little hope of any other commander's being able to lay his vessel in the position taken up by the Queen Charlotte. The French engineers have tripled the extent of the walls, and have substituted for the ancient fortifications a continuous wall two miles long, protected by bastions. In place of a small harbour, covering a superficies of from 10 to 12 acres, which was sufficient for the Algerine corsairs, the jetty constructed by the French engineers affords an anchorage ground extending over 50 acres, and which, when completed, will enclose 250 acres, sufficient for the purposes of a considerable fleet. The military hospital constructed by the French is an immense establishment, beautifully situated in what was formerly the dey's garden, about a mile from the town. There were nine great mosques, and fifty smaller ones in Algiers. Its finest public buildings were the five cassarias, which serve as barracks. But the French pulled down the principal mosque to make room for a new square, and have erected public offices and other edifices in European style. The houses are now numbered, and the streets have received names, amongst which are the Rue Annibal, Orleans, Belisaire, Sidney Smith, Mamides, Lotophages, &c. In the subjoined sketch of the city, previous to its occupation by the French, the Emperor's fort is at G; the city walls are in the line b b b; the line of the aqueduct, cc; the Jenina or dey's palace at <math>D</math>; the harbour at <math>C</math>; the lighthouse at <math>A</math>; and the mole is indicated by h h h. Algiers is said to have contained upwards of 20,000 houses previous to its recent bombardment and storm. A century ago, Shaw estimated the pop. at 100,000; some authorities, however, amongst whom was Consul Shaler, rated the population as low as 50,000 souls. In February 1838, the town and suburbs contained 28,962 inhabitants, of whom 5,575 were Christians, 12,322 Mahommedans, 6,065 Jews, and about 3,000 Kabyles, Negroes, &c. After its conquest by the French, about one-third of the inhabitants migrated. —This city, which is supposed by some to occupy the site of the ancient Jol-Casarea, was first called Musgunna by the Moors, who afterwards named it, says Leo Africanus, "Gezeir, because it lieth near the isles of Minorca, Majorca, and Iviza." There can be little doubt, however, that the appellation Al-Jazerah, or Al-Jezerrat-al-Gazzi, i. e. 'Algiers the warlike,' was derived from the rocky islet before the town. Steamers now ply twice a-week between A. and Marseilles; and London news can be had on the sixth day.—The most interesting and remarkable objects in the environs of the town are the fountains and the marabouts or hermitages.

ALGIERS (BAY OF), an inlet of the Mediterranean, on the Algerine shore, between two headlands lying nearly E and W of each other, viz., Cape Matifu, Matafuz, or Temendfuz, on the E, and Ras Akonnadda, Cape Caxine, or the more eastern Point Pescada, on the W; about 12 m. wide, and 6 m. deep. At the bottom of the bay, the Haratch river, descending from the mountains of Beni Musa, discharges itself. In the month of December Dr. Wilde found this river a muddy stream, scarcely deep enough to float a yacht's gig in. The banks were clothed with willows, oleanders, reeds, and cacti; and the marshes which bordered the river on either side literally swarmed with snipe. On the W side is the city of Algiers, with a lighthouse upon its mole, in N lat. 36° 47′ 20″, E long. 3° 4′ 10″ [Berard], N lat. 36° 48′ 16′, E long. 3° 01′ 19″ [W. H. Smyth]. At the entrance of the bay, the water is about 40 fathoms, shoaling gradually to 15 and 10 near the S shore. Off Cape Matifu, are a few rocks, which show themselves; and about the head of the mole are some others. Off Cape Caxine are two rocks, rising above water, with a passage between them sufficient for small vessels to pass through. The anchorage is excellent, in deep water, on a bottom of stiff black mud; but the bay is exposed to all winds from ENE round northerly to NW. The sea, which tumbles in with northerly gales, is prodigious, in consequence of the abruptness of the coast, which from about 25 fath. depth at the anchorage, falls to 120 fath. outside of it; and at 4 miles in the offing Captain Smyth found no bottom with 650 fath. of line. In the subjoined sketch, Point Pescada is designated by a; the city of Algiers by B; the mole, c; the Emperor's fort, d; batteries, eee; the mouth of the Haratch, f, at the entrance of which the French have erected two forts; water fort, g; and Cape Matifu, h. ALG h B do g The eastern side of the bay forms a contrast with the western; for while the country in the immediate vicinity of the city is picturesquely studded with villas and gardens, embosomed in groves of olive-trees, -on the E side neither houses nor plantations enliven the prospect.
ALGINET, a town of Spain, in Valencia, 8 m. NE by E of Carlet.
ALGINSKOI SYRT, a broken series of high levels which connects the S extremity of the Ural range with the chain of Altai.
ALGIOGY, a v. of Transylvania, in the co. of Hunyad, 15 m. ENE of Deva, on a small tributary of the Maros.

ALGOA BAY, a large indentation of the coast of Uitenhage district, in the Cape colony of South Africa, between Cape Recife and Cape Padrao. From the low rocky point of Cape Recife (a), in S lat. 34° 2', E long. 25° 41', the coast runs N 32° W 4 m. to Beacon point (b); thence NW by N 3 m. to Fort Frederick or Markham's cove (c); thence N 13° E 4 m. to the mouth of the Ferreira (d), which is closed by a sand-bar, except at spring-tides; thence NE by <math>E \neq E 2</math> m. to the mouth of the Zwartkops (e), which is a large river easily accessible to vessels of light tonnage, and formerly gave name to the bay; thence to the Kuga or Coega river (f), off the mouth of which lies Jahleel or North-west island (g), 5 m.; and thence to the mouth of Sunday river, E 4° 35' S, 9 m. A small island called Santa Cruz, about 21 m. in circumf., lies 6 m. S by W from the mouth of the Sunday. It was discovered by Diaz in 1486. From the mouth of Sunday river the coast, now become altogether wild and inhospitable, trends eastward towards Woody cape, in S lat. 33° 461', E long. 26° 13'; and thence to Cape Padrone, in S lat. 33° 461', E long. 26° 24½', 7 m. to the SW of the mouth of Boshman's river. About 5 m. S of Woody cape is a cluster of islets called the Bird islands, by the Portuguese the Chaos or Flat isles, on which the Doddington was wrecked. The anchorage of Port Elizabeth, between Rocky point and Ferreira river, is the best in the bay, presenting good holding-ground, and for six months of the year, during the prevalence of the NW winds, perfectly secure. SE winds prevail most between November and February. The tide rises from 6 to 7 ft. The new town of Port Elizabeth (E) lies 3 m. N of Cape Recife. Uitenhage, the cap. of the district, is about 18 m. NW by N from the port. The country around the bay abounds with game, such as bucks, pheasants, partridges, hares, quails, snipes, and KERRII SKAARKALLES FILLANDALLES FILLANDE FILIS ALF ALF FELT ET ET ET ET ET ET ET ET ET ET ET ET ET wild ducks. The exports from the bay consist of ivory, gum, hides, skins, butter, soap, cheese, aloes, fat, salted meat, salt, and ostrich-feathers. The bay is much frequented by black whales, and its islands are covered with seals. In 1821 the exports were £1,500; in 1841 they were £71,242; and in the first half of 1842, £75,804. This rapid rise is attributed to the extraordinary growth of wool, which is represented as increasing here ten times as fast as in Australia. The land too equals in value that of Australia, and is one quarter the price. The wheat is stated to be the finest in the world, and heavier, by 3 or 4 lbs. per bushel, than the best English wheat. The pop. of the adjacent country is now 70,000.
ALGODANALES, or ALGONALES (Los), a cluster of islets off the coast of Peru, in S lat. 21° 56', W long. 72° 50'.

ALGONQUINS, a numerous family of N. American Indians. once spread over all the northern part of the continent E of the Rocky mountains and S of the St. Lawrence. Their language was "the mother tongue of those who greeted the colonists of Raleigh at Roanoke, and of those who welcomed the pilgrims to Plymouth. It was heard from the bay of Gaspe to the valley of the Des Moines; from Cape Fear, and it may be from the Savannah, to the land of the Esquimaux; from the Cumberland river of Kentucky to the southern bank of the Mississippi. It was spoken, though not exclusively, in a territory that extended through 60 degrees of long., and more than 20 degrees of lat." [Bancroft.] All the tribes of New England were Algonquins; the tribes in Maine claimed the same origin; and the great tribe of the Delaware Indians, the Crees in the region of Slave lake, and the Ottawas and Potawatomies in Michigan. Even among the tribes of Texas, there are warriors who trace their lineage to the Algonquins. The course of the Mississippi from its source in the Rocky mountains to its mouth in Hudson's bay, divided the Athapascas on its l. or N bank from the Algonquins on its r. On the E they stretched along the Atlantic coast from the mouth of the St. Lawrence to the vicinity of Cape Hatteras. A line drawn from the latter cape to the confluence of the Ohio and the Mississippi formed their southern limits, but included the Iroquois, a distinct nation. On the W, their general limit was the Mississippi; and on the NW the Red river separated from the Sioux. Within the limits thus traced, traces of the primitive Algonquin language appear in the names of places,—such as Alleghany, Connecticut, Monengahella, Muskingam; and ethnographical analogies are not wanting in support of their own tradition that at a far distant period they inhabited a remote country lying to the west, whence, migrating eastwards across the Mississippi, they drove before them a numerous and more civilized people, who finally fled down the shores of the Mississippi.
ALGORA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, 32 m. NE of Guadalaxara.
ALGOZO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Trasos-Montes, 20 m. W of Miranda. Pop. 600.
ALGUADA REEF, a cluster of rocks near the western mouth of the Irawaddi, in S lat. 15° 41', E long. 94° 11\f'.
ALGUSA, or Linosa, an uninhabited island in the Mediterranean, 24 m. N of Lampedusa. It is about 6 m. in circuit, and evidently of volcanic origin.

AL-HADR, the name given to a mass of ruins in the desert of Sinjár, in the pash. of Bagdad, occupying a space of ground upwards of a mile in diameter, enclosed by a circular wall of immense thickness, with square bastions at every 60 paces, and measuring 5,460 yards. The whole is formed of a coarse brownish grey limestone, abounding with marine shells, and so closely fitted that, if cement has been used, it cannot be traced. These singular ruins are described by Dr. Ross in the 9th vol., and by Mr. Ainsworth in the 11th vol. of the Geographical Journal.

ALHAMA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Granada, 30 m. SW of Granada. Pop. 4,600. It stands on the brink of a stupendous tajo or fissure, through which the river Marchan forces its way towards the great plain of Granada, and commands the only practicable road across the sierra in this quarter. The streets are narrow, and the houses poor. The only manufacture is that of woollen cloth. There are sulphuretted mineral baths here, which have for ages been in high repute, as indeed is indicated by the name, which is a corruption of the Moorish Al-Ham- | Basilicata, 27 m. E of Potenza. ALI mám. The springs are about a mile from the town. The source which supplies the baths has a temp. of 110°. The soil of the environs is stony, upon a limestone rock, and the cultivation is chiefly confined to that of grain.—Also a town in New Castile, 8 m. S of Calatayad, which is likewise celebrated for its medicinal springs and baths.—Also a town in the prov. of Murcia, 21 m. WSW of Murcia, likewise provided with baths. Pop. 3,800.

ALHAMBRA, or Alambra, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 48 m. E by S of Ciudad Real.—Also a town in Aragon, on a river of the same name, 8 m. N of Teruel. The river rises on the eastern border of the prov., and flowing first to the NW, and then sweeping round to the S, joins the Turia or Albari at Teruel, whence the united streams assume the name of the Guadalaviar.—Also a celebrated Moorish palace. See Granada.

ALHANDRA, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, on the r. bank of the Tagus, 18 m. N by E of Lisbon. Pop. 1,985.—Also a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 9 m. SE of Paraiba, on the Capibari river. Pop. 1,000, chiefly Indians.
ALHAURIN, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, situated on a gentle rise among more lofty eminences, 12 m SW of Malaga. It is much frequented by the Malagarians during the hot season. Pop. 1,000.
ALHO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, on the Capibaribe river, 38 m. W of Olinda.
ALHOLVEDROS, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 8 m. SE of Lisbon.
ALHORN, a town of Oldenburg, in the bailiwick of Wildeshausen. Pop. 503.

ALHUCEMAS, a Spanish presidio on the N coast of Africa, in N lat. 35° 16', 18 leagues W of Melilla, and 30 S of Malaga, which faces it on the coast of Spain. It is a rock of small dimensions, rising in the bay formed by Cape Quillates and Cape Moro, at the extremity of the province of Rif, the most westerly of the kingdom of Marocco. The town is of an irregular shape, and built on a plane inclining from E to W, the level of which varies from one extremity to the other by 40 varas. It is 194 varas in length; its greatest breadth is 98, and its circumference 501. The rock on which it is seated is naturally inaccessible from the N and E; on the W side it is guarded by two batteries, which command the sea and the surrounding country as far as near the Wad Nacer; on the S are three bulwarks protected by two curtains covered with masonry, whence troops may keep up a continual fire under shelter from the enemy.
ALHUE, a town of Chili, in the district of Colchagua, 50 m. SW of Santiago.

ALI, a town of Sicily, on the E coast, 15 m. SSW of Messina.—Also a town of Russian Georgia, 50 m. W of Tiflis.—Also a v. of Persia, in Mazanderan, on the southern descent of the watershed between the streams flowing N to the Caspian, and S towards the plain of Persia.

ALIA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Ciudad Real, 9 m. E of Guadaloupe.—Also a v. of Sicily, 30 m. SSE of Palermo. ALIABAD, [i.e. 'Ali's abode,'] a v. of Persia in Azerdbijan.—Also a v. in Mazanderan, on the Talar river.—Also a v. in the prov. of Irakadjemi, 85 m. NE of Senna.—Also a v. in the delta of the Indus, nearly opposite the New Mall branch.-Also a v. in Western Afghanistan, on the Adruscund river.
ALIABILI, an island in the Caspian, near the W coast, in N lat. 39° 5'.
ALIAGA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 25 m. NE of Temel, on the Guadaloupe.—Also a Yezedee v. of Kurdistan, 32 m. E of Elkosh.
ALIANELLO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of
ALIANO, a town of Naples, 2 m. W of Gaeta, in the district of Lavora.
ALIANSKOI, a fortress of Russia, in the prov. of Kholyvan, and gov. of Tobolsk, 120 m. SW of Kholyvan, in N lat. 52° 50'.
ALIASKA. See ALASHKA.
ALIBAKELL, a watering place on the road from Tajurrah to Ankober, in Abyssinia, 3 days NE of the city of Harrar.
ALIBAUG, an elevated plain of Persia, in Azerdbijan, at the sources of the Zab. Colonel Monteith estimated its highest point at 7,500 ft.
ALIBEG KERI, a town of Bulgaria, 24 m. E of Silistria.
ALIBEGLI, a large v. of Persia, upon the Gáder river, in Azerdbijan.
ALIBEI, a lake of Moldavia, near the coast of the Black sea, 30 m. S by W of Akerman.
ALI BOGHAN, a town in Afghanistan, 10 m. E of Jellalabad, in N lat. 34° 20'; at an elevation of 1,911 ft. above sea-level.
ALI BUNDER, a town of Sinde, on the Gonni, a branch of the Indus, now nearly filled up by alluvial deposits; in N lat. 24° 18'.
ALICANIA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Sonora, 62 m. NW by W of Culiacao.

ALICANT, a modern administrative division of Spain, consisting of the southern district of the ancient kingdom of Valencia, and of a small portion of that of Murcia; bounded on the N by the division of Jativa; on the E and SE by the Mediterranean; on the SW by the prov. of Murcia; and on the W by the division of Chinchilla. It is about 78 m. in its greatest length from N to S; and 75 m. in its greatest width; but its superficies does not exceed 1,800 sq. m. The climate is one of the finest in Europe; winter is here almost unknown; even on the highest summits snow is scarcely ever seen, while the heat of summer is tempered by the sea-breezes. Considerable malaria, however, is occasioned by the numerous lagunes which skirt the coasts of the southern districts. The northern part is traversed by secondary ranges of the Sierra Morena, which decline towards the Mediterranean, and terminate in the capes San Antonio and Escaletes. Some of the higher summits are covvered with majestic forests; while their lower acclivities are industriously cultivated by the inhabitants. The southern portion presents a generally level surface, intersected by low ranges of chalky hills, and streams which descend from the higher regions of the N. In the vicinity of the capital it is wild and broken. The uncultivated ground is covered with the esparto rush, junipers, pines, rosemary, thyme, mint, sage, and lavender, the cistus, and the caper. The soil generally is sandy, with beds of clay and marl; that of the plains, consisting chiefly of rich clay intermingled with fine sand, is of extraordinary fertility, and rewards the intelligent industry of its cultivators with luxuriant crops of rice, oranges, citrons, figs of the finest quality, almonds, olives, dates, with all the pot-herbs of equinoctial climates, and considerable quantities of cotton and of sugar. Brandy is extensively manufactured, and wines of excellent quality: those of the environs of Alicant are particularly esteemed, being "rich with a rough taste combined with sweetness." [Ford.] In the huerta of A. the land never rests: barley being sown in September and reaped in the end of April, when maize is put in which comes off about the middle of September; while some esculent crop is also taken from the same ground. The culture of silk-worms and bees constitutes an important branch of industry. Grana or kermes are gathered on the oak-trees in the environs of Buzots, a few leagues from the city of A. Linen, fine cloth, soap, and paper, are manufactured. The fisheries of 14,230; Ford estimates it at 25,000; but the British ALI tunny and pilchard are very productive.—The district is subdivided into the 16 partidos judiciales of Albaida, Alcoy, Alicant, Altea, Callosa-de-Ensarria, Callosade-Segura, Consentaina, Denia, Elche, Gandia, Jijona, Monovar, Novelda, Onteniente, Oribuela, and Pago; and these are again subdivided into 206 pueblos.-The pop. in 1833 was estimated at 368,923.

ALICANT, the cap. of the above district, is a maritime town of great antiquity, the principal port of Valencia, and, next to those of Cadiz and Barcelona, the most active port in the kingdom. It is situated in a bay of the Mediterranean, in N lat. 38° 20½ and W long. 0° 26' [Espinosa], N lat. 38° 21', W long. 0° 27' [Roper]; 72 m. SSW of Valencia, and 270 S of Madrid. The town stands near the centre of the bay, "on the middle of a narrow neck of land that runs out into the sea a considerable way, and almost comes round in a semicircular form" [Swinburne]; and is defended by a castle, which rises, on an adjacent calcareous rock, to the height of 400 ft. above the level of the sea. Behind this hill is an extensive plain called Las Huertas, i. e., 'the Gardens,' lying along the seashore, and surrounded on three sides by lofty mountains. This huerta is watered from a vast reservoir formed between two mountains 5 leagues from A. The finer sorts of Alicant and Tent wines are made from the produce of this plain. The houses of the town are generally solidly built with flat roofs covered with cement. Their walls too are plastered, and "every thing is as white as the soil of the adjacent country." [Swinburne.] The streets are narrow and irregular, but the houses are generally well-built. The principal public buildings are a collegiate and four parishchurches, two hospitals, six alms-houses, a naval academy, an orphans' school, and an academy for the children of indigent military officers. The port, or roadsted rather, is an open bay stretching from Cape de la Huerta to Cape San Pablo-the former of which bears NE & E 10 m. from the latter—and varies in depth from 4 to 15 fathoms. It is entirely free of bars or shallows, and without any perceptible tide or current. The pier admitting small craft only, the usual line of anchorage is about a quarter of a mile from the shore, and is exposed to winds from ENE to S by W; but a mole is in progress of construction which it is designed to carry out into 24 ft. water. The principal manufactures of the town are cotton and linen fabrics, and soap, the quality of which is held in high repute. The environs produce excellent wine. The export trade, which—notwithstanding the disadvantages of political misrule and prohibitory laws and assessments-continues extremely active, consists in the various articles of provincial produce, as soap, wine, wool, raisins, salt, barilla, kermes, aniseed, antimony, alum, &c., for which it forms an entrepot, and the value of which was at one time estimated at the annual amount of £2,000,000. In 1842 the foreign exports amounted to only £137,313. In the same year, of 229 vessels which entered inwards at this port, with a total tonnage of 51,958 tons, 81 vessels of a total tonnage of 9,265 tons were British, and the invoice value of their cargoes was £39,319; while that of their outward cargo was £35,218. In 1784 the value of British imports was £75,243; of exports £89,344. Its foreign imports are hardware from England, linen from Genoa and France, with cod-fish from Newfoundland, and tobacco from the United States of America. Two extensive salt-lakes between A. and Carthagena afford, by natural evaporation in the dry season, large quantities of salt. A. is the residence of consuls-general for Prussia and Sweden, and of consuls for Austria, England, Russia, Denmark, Tuscany, and the Papal states. Its pop. in 1810 was 21,463; in 1830 it was returned at only consul, in 1842, estimated it at only 11,000, while reporting that "the trade of this and the other ports of the prov. has declined very much during late years both as to importation and exportation generally," owing to the heavy duties. The exportation of raisins only had increased greatly within a few years, amounting in 1841 to 192,000 cwt., and chiefly to Great Britain. In 1784, the export of raisins to this country was 146,560 baskets, valued at £40,304. Steamers usually accomplish the voyage betwixt this port and Valencia in 12 hours.—The Moorish general Abdelasis took this town in 715. Ferdinand II. reunited it to the kingdom of Murcia in the 13th cent. In 1304 it was ceded to the king of Aragon. In the war of succession it adhered to Philip V. In the 16th cent. it received a great accession of pop. from the number of families who took refuge in it to escape the frequent ravages of the Algerines. In 1706, the castle was taken by the English. A great part of it was afterwards blown up: the English garrison, though apprized of the mine being ready to be sprung, refusing to capitulate. When Townsend visited A. in 1787, large shattered portions of the rock seemed still to be threatening the city with destruction. In 1813, defended by a British fleet, it successfully resisted the French general Suchet. — Bourgoing. — Swinburne. — Townsend.—Commercial Tariff.—Ford. ALICATA—contracted Licata—a town of Sicily, situated on a promontory at the mouth of the Salso, on the S coast of the Val di Mazzara, in the prov. and 24 m. ESE of Girgenti; in N lat. 37° 4', E long. 13° 55½'. Pop. 13,465. It is built partly on the beach, partly on the slope of the adjacent hills, and protected by two forts; but its walls have gone to decay, and lie in fragments on the beach, and the general appearance of the town is that of a neglected and decaying place. The adjacent plains are, however, well-cultivated and fertile; though Simond speaks of A..as "bordering on a desert." Grain and sulphur are the chief exports. Boats can pass up the river, but at its entrance is a shoal of nearly a mile in length on which the surf beats heavily with S winds; and the usual anchorage, a mile to the SW of the town, in from 7 to 12 fath., is also much exposed to winds from W by S round southwardly to E.—This town is generally supposed to be built on the site of the ancient Gela; but Cluverius places that town at Terranova.—Swinburne.—Smyth.—Hoare.
ALICE (CAPO DELL'), a promontory of Naples, on the Calabrian coast, on the W side of the gulf of Toranto, 13 m. E of Umbriatico. It was the Crimissan promontory of the ancients.
ALICIA, an island on the W coast of the gulf of Boothia, in N lat. 70° 50'.

ALICUDI, the most western island of the Lipari group, 56 m. ENE of Palermo; in N lat. 38° 32½, E long. 14° 16½. It is about 6 m. in circum., and rises abruptly from the sea like a conical crater. Pop. 260.
ALIF (Point), a long narrow strip of land forming the N point of the island of Massera in the Persian gulf, on which a v. of the same name stands.— Also a Turkoman v. in the pashalik of Angora.

ALIFE, a town of Naples, in the Terra di Lavora, on the l. bank of the Volturno, 15 m. NW of Capua. Pop. 1,836. It occupies the site of the Alifa or Allifæ of the Romans. Berar, 68 m. SW of Ellichpur.

ALIGHUR, a district of India, in the doab of the Ganges and the Jumna, lying to the S of the district of Merut. The fort of A. is 53 m. N of Agra. It is a strong place, and was taken by the British with severe loss in 1803.
ALIGNAN-DU-BENT, a com. of France, in the dep. of Herault, arrond. of Beziers. Pop. 1,154. ALI
ALIGUAI, a low wooded islet off the N coast of Mindanao, in the Philippine group, in N lat. 8° 40', E long. 123° 10′.
ALIKA, a v. of Greece, in Laconia, near Cape Matapan.
ALIKHOOLIP (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of Patagonia, in S lat. 55° 12', W long. 70° 48'.
ALIMENA, a cant. and town of Sicily, in the prov. of Palermo, 18 m. N by E of Caltanisetta. Pop. 3,376.
ALIMPAPON, a town on the W coast of the island of Mindanao, in N lat. 7°.

ALI MUSJID, a fort of Afghanistan, in the Khyber pass, 8 m. from its E entrance. The width of the pass is here only 150 yards; alt. above sea-level 2,433 ft. It was captured by the British in July 1839; but of 2,442 men who were posted here, no fewer than 243 died in 57 days, from the unwholesomeness of the water it is supposed. It was held by 150 of the Eusufzai tribe for the British from Nov. 1841 to January following, when the garrison, reinforced by two regiments, succeeded in evacuating the fort. On the final evacuation of the country in 1842 this fort was entirely destroyed.
ALINAGHUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, 20 m. N of Garipur.—Also a town in the Carnatic, 10 m. W of Tricolur.

ALINE (LOCH), a beautiful inlet of the sound of Mull, in the Morven district of the coast of Argyle. It is connected with the sound by a very narrow channel, and penetrates about 2 m. into the country.

ALINGAR, a river of Afghanistan, which rises in the Hindukush, and, running SW through a fertile valley in the district of Lughman, joins the Alishang after a course of about 100 m. The united streams, after a course of 25 m., fall into the Kabul river.
ALINGHERY, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 38 m. SW of Arcot.
ALINGSAES, a town of Sweden, 35 m. SSE of Wenersborg, on the Saveä, near its entrance into the Mioren lake. Pop. in 1833, 1,221.
ALINSKOE, a salt lake of the Crimea, near a rocky point projecting into the sea of Azof called Kafandip. It is 23 versts in circumference, and has evidently originated from a bay of the sea.—Pallas.
ALIPATOK, an island in Hudson's strait, at the entrance of Ungava bay, on the coast of Labrador; in N lat. 60°, W long. 67°.

ALIPEE, a town of Hindostan, in Travancore, which Milburn places in N lat. 9° 30', E long. 76° 34', near a river which has communication with that of Quilon. Horsburgh places Porea in this position; but adds that there is a v. called Alipee, 3 leagues to the N. Buckingham, on the other hand, states that the natives on this coast were unacquainted with the name Porea; but said that the town was called Alipelly or Alipee. It is about 40 m. from Cochin. Pop. 13,000.
ALIPORA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Allahabad, 15 m. SW from Jeitpur.
ALIPUR, a v. in the Punjab, on the road from Surinkpur to Ferozepur.—Also a v. of Scinde, on the l. bank of the Indus.
ALIPUTA, a town of Ceylon, in N lat. 6° 54', 53 m. SE from Candy.
ALISE-SAINTE-REINE, a com. and v. of France,

ALIGAUM, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, arrond. and 7 m. NE of Semur. Pop. 776. There are iron-mines and mineral springs in the vicinity. The v. is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Alesia.
ALISHA, a v. of Persia, in Azerdbijan, in N lat. 38° 8′, 24 m. from Tabriz.

ALISHAN, a v. of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Diarbekir, 4 hours ride E by S from Mezirah, 2 hours S from the Murad river. The environs are well-cultivated, and produce grain, cotton, and the Palma Christi.

ALISHANG, or ALISHENG, a river of Afghanistan, which, rising in the Nadjil on the S slope of the Hindukush, flows S, and joins the Alingar at Tirgaree, after a course of about 90 m. through a narrow valley.
ALISSAS, a com. of France, dep. of Ardeche. Pop. 898.
ALISTRATI, a town of Turkey, in the prov. of Galipoli, 5 m. W by N of Zigna.
ALI TA'GH, a mountain-range of Asia Minor, in Karamania, lying NNE of the Bulghár Tágh, and rising above the line of perpetual snow. It is called ALLAH DAGH in Kiepert's map.
ALI TSHELEBI, a town of Greece, in the Morea, NE of Gastuni.
ALIXAN, or ALISSAN, a com. of France, in the dep. of La Drome, arrond. of Valence. Pop. 2,489.

ALIWAL, a v. of India, in the Punjab, 17 m. W by N of Lodiana, near the l. bank of the Sutledge. After the successive defeat of the Sikhs at Moodkee and Ferozepore, and their retreat upon Sobraon, Sir Harry Smith, on the 28th of January, 1846, encountered near this v. a Sikh force of 24,000 men under Runjut Singh, who had crossed the river and was threatening Lodiana. The action began about 10 A. M., and by 1 P. M. the Sikh army was driven from its entrenched position-of which A., covering the ford by which they had crossed, was the key-with the loss of their whole park of 68 guns, and from 5,000 to 6,000 men.
ALIZAY, a com. of France, in the dep. of the Eure, arrond. of Louviers, intersected by the Paris and Rouen railway. Pop. 570.
ALIZYE, a v. in Afghanistan, in N lat. 30° 40′, 40 m. N of Quetta.
ALJAMILLA (SIERRA DE), a ramification of the Sierra de Filabres, in the prov. of Almeria, in Granada, terminating on the coast in Cape Gata.
ALJEZUR, a town of Portugal, in Algarve, in the comarca and 18 m. NNW of Lagos, on a small stream of the same name.
ALJIRACA, a river of Upper Peru, in the prov. of La Paz, flowing from the Andes into the Chuquiabo.
ALJUBARROTA, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 15 m. SW of Leiria. Pop. 1,600. John I. of Portugal defeated John I. of Castile in the environs of this town on the 14th of August, 1385.
ALJUCEN, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. of Badajoz, on a small river of the same name which flows into the Guadiana, 9 m. below Merida.
ALJUSTREL, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 12 m. SSE of Leiria.—Also a town in the prov. of Alemtejo, 10 m. S of Ferreira. Pop. 1,500.

ALKAM, a town of Lower Egypt, on the Nile, nearly opposite to Nadir, at the point where a canal commencing at Haou, on the l. bank of the Nile near Dendera, and running parallel with the river under the successive names of Moye, Sonhadj, and Bahr- Yussuff, enters the Rosetta branch of the Nile.
ALKEN, a town of Jutland, at the NE end of Lake Moss, 3 m. NE of Skanderberg.—Also a v. of Belgium, in the prov. and 8 m. NW of Liege.
ALKERTON, a parish in Oxfordshire, 6 m. WNW of Banbury. Area 650 acres. Pop. 190.
ALKHAM, a parish in Kent, 4 m. W by N of Dover. Area 3,190 acres. Pop. 595.

ALKMAAR, a strongly fortified town of Holland, in the prov. of N. Holland, on the Helder canal, 20 m. NNW of Amsterdam; in N lat. 52° 38', E long. 4° 44'. It is a snug-looking, clean town, intersected with canals, the banks of which are adorned with trees. The surrounding meadows are of the richest pasturage; and vast quantities of butter and cheese ALL are disposed of in the markets of A., which has also a considerable trade in sail-cloth, cattle, corn, seeds, and flower-roots. It is noted in the city-records that, in 1637, a lot of 120 tulips with their offsets was sold here for 90,000 florins. In 1796, the pop. of A. was 8,373, of whom the greater part were returned as Catholics. In 1830 the pop. was 9,439.—The Spaniards, after the capture of Haerlem in 1573, laid siege to this place but were unable to take it.

AL-KOSH, a town in Kurdistan, in N lat. 36° 54', E long. 42° 59', 8 hours N of Mozul, 2 days from Amadia, and 3 from Urmiah. It stands on a ridge of hills which runs between Akra and Doban; and is entirely inhabited by a sturdy tribe of Chaldean Catholics. About a mile higher up the ridge is the ancient convent of Rabban Hormuzd, "a heavy square building of a dusky colour, hanging quite over a precipice." [Rich.] The Tigris is visible SW from the convent, and in a NW direction from the summit of the mountain. Wolff says, "the grave of the prophet Nahum is here, to which the Jews from Moussul and Bagdad perform their pilgrimage."
AL-KURIAT ISLANDS, a group of islets off the E coast of Tunis, 12 leagues S by E & E from Ras-al- Mahmur. They are sometimes called the ConigLiera ISLANDS.
ALKRINGTON, a township and v. in the parish of Oldham-cum-Prestwick, 41 m. N by E of Manchester. Pop. 338.
ALLA, a town of Bornu, 60 m. S of Kouka, in N lat. 12° 12′.
ALLAGASH. See ALAGUASH.
ALLAGNA DI SESIA, a town of Piedmont, 18 m. NW of Varallo. Pop. 1,600. Copper-mines are wrought in the neighbourhood.
ALLAGNAT, a com. of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of Clermont. Pop. 880.

ALLAHABAD, an extensive prov. of Hindostan Proper, between the 24th and 26th parallels of N lat., and bounded on the N by the prov. of Agra and of Oude; on the E by Bahar; on the SE and S by the Hindu prov. of Gundwana; and on the W by Mulwa and Agra. Its length from E to W is estimated at about 270 m., and its average breadth from N to S at 120 m. The principal divisions of the prov., named zillahs, are the following: Allahabad, which contains the city and fortress of the same name; Juanpur; Benares; Mirzapur, each of which three districts likewise possesses towns of the same name of considerable importance; the Rewah territory; Bundelkund; Cawnpore; and the Manichpore territory. The northern portion of the prov., watered by the Jumna, Ganges, Sye, Goomty, Caramnassa, and by numerous smaller streams, presents, in the vicinity of the two first-mentioned rivers, a generally level surface; but towards the SW it rises into elevated plateaus, intersected by ranges of high hills, with comparatively few streams, but possessing to a considerable extent the same luxuriant fertility which characterises the plains of the N. The Jumna, previous to its junction with the Ganges in this prov., rolls on rapidly between steep banks; but the Ganges flows slowly over sand-banks which rise gradually from the water. Their point of confluence has an alt. of 800 ft. above Calcutta. A canal 75 m. in length has been executed between the Goomty and the Ganges. The temperature of the lower districts is extremely sultry, and liable to the action of hot winds; that of the hilly region is generally more equable and genial. The principal articles exported from this prov. are cotton, cotton fabrics, indigo, saltpetre, opium, diamonds-from the formerly rich mines of Pannah or Punna, in the Bundelkund-sugar, &c. The traffic upon the Jumna is very considerable. Large quantities of cotton are shipped for the Calcutta market at Humirpore, Kupar, Agra, and stations still higher up; the transport of indigo and sugar also employs many vessels. At Chilleh Tarehgaut, a thoroughfare of great traffic, goods of all kinds coming, by way of Mhow, from Bombay on camels, are embarked for the supply of Bengal. Of its imports, salt from the maritime districts of Bengal is now the most important. A. is one of the most populous prov. of Hindostan; the majority of its inhabitants are Hindus, about one-eighth only of their number professing Mahommedanism. It is stated by Abul Fazel, that early in the 11th cent., the territory which now forms the prov. of A. was twice unsuccessfully invaded by Sultan Mahmud of Ghizni. It was subsequently subdued by the Patan emperors of Delhi; and in the 15th cent. became an independent kingdom, the capital of which was Juanpur. It was afterwards constituted a distinct soubah by the emperor Acbar, and received from him the appellation by which it is now distinguished. After the fall of the Mogul dynasty, it was appropriated by the nabob of Oude; and was ultimately ceded by Saadet Ali, at the beginning of the present cent., to the Bengal government.

ALLAHABAD, a judicial subdivision of the prov. of the same name, surrounding in its greater extent the city of A., but stretching in detached portions nearly 100 m. along the banks of the Ganges and Jumna. The soil, consisting chiefly of rich sandy loam, is extremely fertile. The average rent of wheat-land is about £1 per acre. Barley, pease, and potatoes are grown in considerable quantities; and since 1801 the produce of opium, cotton, and indigo has very greatly augmented. In 1815 about 550,000 acres were under cultivation, and yielded an assessment of 2,793,244 rupees; but large tracts, many of which might be rendered highly productive, are still completely waste. The breed of sheep in this district is of small size, and the fleece consists of a coarse, black, lank wool, used only in the manufacture of shepherds' rugs. The quantity of cotton-cloths and chintzes formerly manufactured in Currah and Shahzadpur is now greafly diminished; but the baftaes and sullums of Allahabad and Futtehpoor are still in considerable demand. The transit-trade from the lower provinces consists chiefly in Sambher salt, unwrought iron, cotton, and shawls. Although presenting a progressively increasing revenue, this district, in consequence of originally exorbitant assessments, and of the transfer to adversely interested parties of large portions of its territory, has greatly failed to realize the estimate that on its first acquisition it seemed capable of sustaining. The usual dress of the peasantry consists of merely a piece of coarse cloth tied round the middle, and a woollen shawl; the head is protected by a sort of turban of cotton cloth. ALLAHABAD, [i. e. 'the Abode of Allah,'] a fortified city in Hindostan Proper, the cap. of the prov. of the same name, situated on the extreme point of the Doab, or at the junction of the Ganges and Jumna; in "perhaps the most favourable situation which India affords for a great city," [Heber], in N lat. 25° 27', E long. 81° 50'; at the distance of 550 m. in a direct line, but 820 m. by the course of the river, from the sea, 53 m. from Benares, 127 m. from Lucknow, 296 m. from Agra, and 212 m. from Delhi. The fort, "once unequalled in beauty, and now gaining in strength what it has lost in external appearance," [Asiatic Journ.] occupies a strong position on a tongue of land formed by the confluence of the above-mentioned rivers, the navigation of which it completely commands. It is of great extent, and is defended towards the rivers by bastioned walls built of polished freestone. The fortifications of the inner strength. The principal gateway is of Grecian architecture, and possesses considerable elegance. The whole building "still retains somewhat of its Oriental and feudal air, and may be espied at a very considerable distance." [Asiatic Journ.] The interior is finely planted. In the dry season, the water is so low that the fort is accessible from numerous sandbanks in the Ganges, the haunts of legions of alligators. The military cantonments are situated at a considerable distance from the fort. They are beautifully picturesque, having a greater diversity of hill and dale than is usually to be found upon the plains of India, and being finely wooded. The government - house, formerly the regal palace, is pleasant and commodious. The most remarkable edifices still in existence in the vicinity of A., are the serai and mausoleums of Sultan Khosru and his mother. Nine-tenths of the present native buildings of the city consist of superstructures of mud upon the ruins of former splendour. The schools established for the education of native youths are prosperous. A powder-manufactory has been erected in the environs by the government; and the city is rapidly assuming an air of business and prosperity. The commercial rank which A. once possessed, as the great mart for the cotton of the Deccan and of the districts south of the Jumna, has been transferred to Mirzapur; but the advantages of its site constitute it still the emporium of Oude, Bundelkund, and Bogalkund. The principal source of its revenues, however, consists in the pre-eminence which it holds in Hindu veneration as the holiest of their prayagas, or sacred confluences of rivers. The number of pilgrims of all castes who annually repair to A. to bathe themselves in the united streams, is estimated at upwards of 200,000; and a tax of 3 rupees levied on each by the East India Company, produced an average amount of upwards of 700,000 rupees. The situation of A. is said to be healthy; but it possesses a more humid climate than any other place in the Doab. This city is the permanent station of the Sudder commission— a body of judges forming a circuit-court subordinate to the Sudder Dewany Adawlat of Bengal. A. is supposed by D'Anville and by Dr. Robertson to occupy the site of the ancient Palimbothra; but Rennell opposes this opinion. It was a favourite residence of the emperor Acbar, and owed to him most of the magnificence which it formerly possessed. The pop., exclusive of the garrison, was, in 1803, estimated at 20,000. The undulating surface of the country in the environs of A. affords numerous advantageous sites for bungalows, many of which are scattered about here. The Bundelkund bank of the Jumna, opposite A., is rocky, rising in towering cliffs crowned with pagodas and mouldering hill-forts; but the banks of the Ganges here have no particular beauty beyond its common features. The navigation of the river, which was formerly greatly impeded by dangerous rocks, has been cleared of these and other obstacles: and boats of the largest size used in inland navigation now pass up and down the rapid stream in safety. The waters of the Jumna, flowing over a rocky and sandy bed, except during the periodical swelling of the stream, are peculiarly pure as contrasted with those of the Ganges, and retain their brilliant blue for a considerable distance after their confluence with the turbid yellow wave with which they are doomed at length to mingle. The water of the Ganges, however, when allowed some little time to clear itself, is by far the most palatable of the two. The water of the Jumna is impregnated with natron.

ALLAHABAD, a station in the Panjal pass, on the side of Kashmir. The birch begins to appear about side are of very regular construction, and of great | this point; and hence downwards to Huripur, the mountains are clothed with pines, while the banks | at its narrowest part. It is preferred by navigators close on each side of the pass.
ALLAHABAD, a town of Bhalpur, 25 m. SW of Ahmedpur, in N lat. 28° 53'.
ALLAHDUND, a considerable town of Northern India, in the Berdurani country, in Lower Swaut.— Elphinstone.
ALLAH-KILISSA, a v. of Turkey, in the sanjak and 27 m. WNW of Salonika. It is supposed to mark the site of the ancient Pella.
ALLAH-SHEHR. See ALAH-SHAHIR.
ALLAINES, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, arrond. of Chartres. Pop. 552.
ALLAIRE, a cant. and com. of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, arrond. of Vannes. Pop. in 1831 of the cant., which comprises 8 com., 11,381; of the town of A., 25 m. SE of Vannes, 1,975 in 1842.
ALLAIS. See ALEIS.

ALLALEIN, one of the great glaciers of Switzerland descending from the Saasgrat, a lofty chain of inaccessible snowy peaks which attain an alt. of 15,000 ft. between the valley of Saas and that of Zermatt, in the Pennine Alps, in the canton of Valais. Professor Forbes has given a plan and section of it in his Travels through the Alps, p. 345.
ALLAMAN, a v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, on the NW side of the lake of Geneva, 12 m. SW of Lausanne.
ALLAMBADY, a town of India, in Mysore, 74 m. ESE of Seringapatam.
ALLAMIENA, a town of Italy, in the States-ofthe-Church, to the NE of Civita Vecchia.
